My [python3 branch](https://github.com/chrysn-pull-requests/pydtls/tree/python3) contains now a mess of changes that might be sufficient to get simple things running. IMO next steps are:

* pydtls project will need to decide on a Python 3 strategy -- whether six or 3to2 or something like that will be used; can't help there, I never maintained a works-for-both library
* Some changes are rather straightforward and (with some six-or-similar support) should also work on Python 2 (eg. replacing ``".join([chr(x) for x in ...])`` with ``bytes(...)``, skipping irrelevant tests on Python 3 or using getsockopt instead of .type to account for blocking information in Python 3's type field)
* Some changes need more consideration; especially, the ``sock._sock`` mechanism is completely different in Python 3 (the socket is the object itself and not its ``._sock`` property; my workaround is happily creating identical socket objects and keeps the unused ones in an artificial memory leak to avoid closing), and needs to be re-thought by someone who actually understands how pydtls is supposed to work and doesn't just put together the bits that break under the unit test.

The above got me far enough to discover that I'll be blocked by https://bugs.python.org/issue19084 with pydtls (no way to use preshared keys (PSK) with Python's ssl). I'll stay subscribed to this bug in case anything around my patches is unclear, but unless I see a roadmap with the underlying PSK issue with ssl is resolved, I won't push this any further on my own.

I'd probably revert to my original stance:

- Task management would NOT be the only thing I will
be dealing on an exception. I should be handling it
properly and providing required communication channels
covering all possible situations in the app.

- If an exception is left unhandled in a task, than that means:
I don't care about the task.

- So the idea of a mechanism for dealing with unhandled
exception is self defeating.

HOWEVER, I'm looking to this:

Doing something here and getting an exception
elsewhere is something interesting. Examples are:

- Cancellation: This is infact Task1 sending an exception
to Task2.
- Timeout: Task1 sends an future exception to itself.

They are something like intertask communication.
Although this is called "exception" (which does not
sound as a good thing) the receiver can receive
it at "Any Awaiting Point" of its execution. (Its for cancellation.)

Well I'm still not sure whether this is a good
thing or not. But this is definetely an emergent property
of asyncs.

This may be something that can be abused
or with some further generalization effort can it be
turned into a interesting expressive construct?

Any thoughts?

...I made a long post about this on the [curio forum](https://forum.dabeaz.com/c/curio/) last night, and now it seems to have disappeared. Or maybe it's trapped in some sort of moderation hades?

@aktiur:
> I feel that, ideally, it should be obvious which end-user facing coroutines only use synchronous traps and are guaranteed in the future to do so.

Agreed -- my end goal is to figure out what guarantees end-user APIs are or aren't guaranteed to provide, but figuring out the right system for traps seems like a good first step :-).

> Maybe curio could make the guarantee that its own async context managers won't block in aenter, and all coroutines that should not block [...] should be converted to context managers?

This is... really weird :-). Context managers already have a clear and well-motivated use case that has nothing to do with synchronous/non-synchronous, and like you note, some of the most important use cases for async context managers are ones that involve blocking in `__aenter__`.

What might make sense to me is to have a rule that whenever we can make a function non-async, we should, because that *is* a clear signal to the user about what it can and can't do. So for example, maybe `Event.set()` should be a regular method, not an async method. (Curio already provides this functionality twice -- `SyncEvent.set()` is a regular method, and `Event.set()` does the weird frame hack thing to let itself be called as a sync method from sync code. AFAICT there's no reason for the `await event.set()` style call to exist.) OTOH, there will still be coroutines like `curio.timeout_after` and `curio.current_task` that are synchronous but are all about talking to the event loop, and I guess the best option for these is just to have a convention that we should document clearly whatever guarantees we settle on.

@dabeaz:
> I think this is being overthought. I don't want to make any guarantee one way or the other about any operation involving async/await other than any such operation might block (or context switch). The operative word here is "might." The fact that certain low-level operations don't block is purely an implementation detail--and one that could change at any moment. It could be different using different kernel implementations or different scheduling priorities. 

As a general principle, I strongly disagree. I started writing an erlang-style task supervisor, and it turns out that this is really annoying if you aren't allowed to even *know* whether `task.cancel` is cancellable. That's why I started poking at this in the first place, because it was making it hard to write code on top of curio. `curio.Event` was recently broken because you accidentally assumed a trap was synchronous and it wasn't. If you're making mistakes like these, it seems only fair to assume that users will as well :-).

Obviously there's a whole substantive conversation to have about exactly which operations should make which guarantees, though. I just think the rule should be more nuanced than "any operation could do anything". That's nice for devs, but not so nice for users. Good APIs have to balance those competing interests, plus things like usability, teachability, etc.

Another general rule for API design is that if the code makes a guarantee but the docs don't, then this is often the worst of all worlds, because user can't count on it, but they end up depending on it anyway, so that the devs lose the freedom to change it. So for example, I like [this note from Linus](http://yarchive.net/comp/linux/child-runs-first.html) mentioning how some quirks of how Linux schedules processes after `fork` ended up being stuck in stone for a while because of a bash bug -- and there they're talking about a fully-preemptive scheduler running on multi-core hardware, where the original "guarantee" was pretty weak to start with. The analogous question for curio would be whether `curio.spawn` runs the child task before returning to the parent task. My preference would be that we document that `spawn` is synchronous (so the parent always runs first), but if we don't do that then I think we'd be better off making it random (like, I mean literally doing `import random` and using that inside the `spawn` implementation) than providing a consistent behavior but refusing to document it.

Also also, even if we, say, go ahead and document that `spawn` is synchronous now, as long as curio remains unstable we can always change our mind later and un-document it :-). But IMO the point of curio being unstable is give us room to figure out what the eventual stable API should look like, so that includes exploring options for what kinds of stability guarantees make sense.

On 12/18/16, David Beazley <notifications@github.com> wrote:
> The curio subprocess functions already kill the underlying subprocess on
> timeout.

Does cancelling the below task kill it? Anyway, curio
user may need a way to kill/term a subprocess
on conditions other than a timeout.

```python
    async def wait(self, timeout=None):
        async def waiter():
            while True:
                retcode = self._popen.poll()
                if retcode is not None:
                    return retcode
                await sleep(0.0005)
        task = await spawn(waiter())
        try:
            return await timeout_after(timeout, task.join())
        except TaskTimeout:
            await task.cancel()
            raise TimeoutExpired(self.args, timeout) from None
```

Yes there is. But without a 'nested' example.

I've always felt that event loop code is minimized by focusing on async/await as the "pluggable" component, not the event loop.   Curio takes this to an extreme in that there is no event loop (or kernel) explicitly involved anywhere in the API other than the top-level run() method to start things.   Specifically, you never carry around a reference the loop/kernel and use it to invoke methods. You also never supply a loop/kernel reference to other objects (i.e., locks, queues, etc.).   For all practical purposes, it simply doesn't exist.   The fact that it doesn't exist means that in theory, something other than curio could probably be swapped in as long as the same async/await API was provided somehow (it wouldn't necessarily matter how it actually worked under the covers).  

Reconciling this with asyncio where the event loop is needed just about everywhere is an interesting problem to think about to be sure.

Of course, keep in mind that Curio can get away with this by going full coroutine.  Nobody goes full coroutine. ;-).

I'm mostly interested in units checking, not so much in conversions and/or having units attached to my variables (e.g. for documentation) With my example from above:
```
volume = 1 * ureg['m**3']
rho = 1.2 * ureg['kg*m**-3']
mass = rho * volume
```
`mass` automatically becomes `<Quantity(1.2, 'kilogram')>` which is correct, but if I had accidentally written `mass = rho / volume` I get the incorrect units for mass. If using the `=` operator we could only change the value of an object (instead of creating a new one) this would be possible:

        :param nb_words: around how many words the sentence should contain

        if variable_nb_words:

Hi @benbowen,

Glad to hear from you, any feedback is much appreciated. Yes, SymPy is a large library indeed.

I recently added the keyword argument `underdetermined` to `balance_stoichiometry`:
http://bjodah.github.io/chempy/latest/chempy.html#chempy.chemistry.balance_stoichiometry

My idea was that if a user sets it to `False` it would disallow (raise an error) under-determined systems. And setting it to `True` (the default) would give the linear degrees of freedoms as SymPy symbols, and setting it to `1` would return an explicit balancing with integer coefficients of minimum magnitude.

When I pass `underdetermined=1` for your system I get this answer:
```
>>> balance_stoichiometry({u'C7H5O3-', u'O2', u'C21H27N7O14P2-2', u'H+'}, {u'C7H5O4-', u'C21H26N7O14P2-', u'H2O'}, underdetermined=1)
({'C21H27N7O14P2-2': 4, 'O2': 3, 'C7H5O3-': 2, 'H+': 4}, {'C21H26N7O14P2-': 4, 'H2O': 4, 'C7H5O4-': 2})
```
so it seems that ChemPy to set `x1 == 4` instead of `x1 == 2` which supposedly is the smallest integer giving integer coefficients here.

So it looks like there is a bug (although somewhat benign). I'll look into it! I also see that the documentation of that function is not quite accurate with respect to `underdetermined`, I'll try to clarify that as well.

In the particular case of RSI, the value 50 is chosen because the indicator is constrained to range between 0 and 100, and it is calculating some averages that are taken over a number of previous periods. The N/A values occur (at least) at the beginning of the data when not enough data is available to compute the indicator for the specified timeframe, so the N/A are optionally replaced with 50, which is effectively “no information” or “neither overbought nor oversold”.

All sorts of variations on how to deal with this sort of situation might be possible, but I’m personally not convinced that there’s a bug here.

Definitely if you have an improvement in mind you can just write the code in your own fork, and potentially propose it for inclusion here too!

That's a fair question.  The fact is that we're making a number of HTTP calls to various endpoints on the same service, as follows:
-  a GET to check the status of something
-  a PUT or a POST depending on the outcome of the first GET, to invoke an operation
-  a polling GET (using `httsleep`!) to monitor the status of the operation
-  two GETs at the end to retrieve the operation's output.

Since we need to pass an auth token header and `verify=False` to each of these, it's cleaner for us to allocate a `Session` like so:
```python
        session = requests.Session()
        session.verify = False
        session.headers.update({'Authorization': 'token=%s' % my_token})
```
and then pass that an everywhere, rather than put the auth header (along with any endpoint-specific headers!) and `verify=False` in every single call we make.

Hi Won,

In the folder "scripts", there is a script called "reads_to_ctg_map.py" that runs the mapping for you. Currently, you can choose either to map with bwa-sampe or bwa-mem. Im assuming you have files like lib1_A.fq, lib1_B.fq, lib2_A.fq, lib2_B.fq  (or fasta format). That is, reads are split up into two files for each library. Then I would run  "reads_to_ctg_map.py" twice like:

python reads_to_ctg_map.py   /path/to/lib1_A.fq  /path/to/lib1_A.fq  /path/to/contigs.fasta  --threads <N> 

python reads_to_ctg_map.py   /path/to/lib2_A.fq  /path/to/lib2_A.fq  /path/to/contigs.fasta  --threads <N>

where N could e.g. be set to the number of cores you have available (it decides the number of threads for bwa-mem). To use bwa-sampe, just add the parameter --nomem. Sorting and indexing the bam-file is performed automatically in this pipeline. BESST is more thoroughly tested by me with the with bwa-sampe pipeline but it also takes longer time to map with this method. That said it does not not necessarily perform worse or better with any one of them. Mappings from Bowtie/bowtie2 could also be used but you would have to performe those steps yourself with the guidance of the bowtie manual on how to map pared reads.

"Mapping reads: If you want to map a mate pair library, you will need to map them as paired end library i.e. forward-reverse mode. All read pair libraries should be in this order. "

This is for an older version of besst. With besst >=1.3 you can map in any orientation. Just make sure when you call besst to set the --orientation parameter. If you have one fr and one rf library (mapped in that order), i.e. a paired end and a mate pair library, you can run besst as:

runBESST -c /path/to/contigfile.fa -f /path/to/file1.bam /path/to/file2.bam -o /path/to/output --orientation fr rf

(See https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/master/docs/MANUAL.md)


Hope this helps and I wish you a productive new year!

@stef the difference is that Value/TypeErrors are easy to catch and present to the user (or next layer) when they are due to user input. They allow you to avoid writing another layer that checks everything and instead write pythonic "EAFP" code.

@stef Like you wrote above, assertions can be disabled at compile time, so they are not equivalent. Relying on catching an AssertionError can lead to bugs.

[EAFP](https://docs.python.org/2/glossary.html#term-eafp) = Easier to Ask for Forgiveness than Permission

Hi, if you want to use a pool of connections you must set ``client_strategy=REUSABLE``. This creates a pool of independent connection to a pool of server, that you can specify in the Connection definition with a ServerPool object instead of a Server object. This is still not well documented on ldap3.readthedocs.org, sorry.

Furthermore Cursors now raises LDAP operation exception only when ``raise_exceptions=True``. If it is set to False you can check the Cursor.failed boolean value. In this way when multiple LDAP operation are performed by a single Cursor operation (for example a commit of many entries) all operations are executed even if one of them returned an error. (Up to 2.2.0 the Cursor stops if an LDAP operation is not successful). 

I think this is a better behaviour.

Everything has credentials, so those credentials are abstracted away from core code, and into a single config file. The project already does this, how *it* handles those credentials when passed in, is _internal_ to the API not the outside world.  Of course, plugins may not share the same type of creds, e.g. some have keys, tokens, usernames, emails, passwords, passkeys, etc etc, but the DRY is that they can be placed in a convenient file, first for your development testing, and then everyone else can follow your tried and tested lead and insert their own creds into the placeholders that you leave  behind (now emptied) in your sample file ;)

The KML schema only mentions `coordinatesType` as being of base type `string`, without any specific mask or facet.

Google KML Reference mentions the need to have a minimum amount of coordinate tuples, and as such, an empty coordinate string should be considered an Exception.

The OGC KML Standard requires `coordinates` to be:

> String representing one or more coordinate tuples

...and as such, an empty string would be also an error.

As a final remark, there are [some other parsers](https://github.com/googlegis/sharpkml) that save "non-recognized elements" in a separate list, so that when you save back to disk (possibly after editing some recognized elements), the original, unrecognized file content is not lost. That is, IMO, a very desireable feature.

Thanks for the PR.  This is a very nice PR.  Hardly anyone does it right on the first time :)  I like the documentation and the tests especially.  

Anyway, there is one issue.  This change has a performance impact for existing use cases.  For example, disjoint_subsets is the main computational component of the segment_image() routine (https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/image_transforms/segment_image.h) and this change will have a significant runtime impact.  If you look at the code you will see that the segment_image() is already doing this same bookkeeping to keep track of the set sizes, so one possible solution is to update segment_image() to use this new bookkeeping inside disjoint_subets.  However, there are other uses that don't require this bookkeeping at all, and more importantly, there are other dlib users out there who use this tool and won't be updating their code just to avoid the set size duplication of computation.

So, a better approach would be to make a `disjoint_subsets_sized` or `disjoint_subsets_with_set_sizes` class that contains an instance of disjoint_subsets or inherits from it and provides this additional functionality.  This way existing uses of the class don't pay the runtime overhead for this, but new uses can use it if appropriate.

The keyring framework already has priorities for the backends and it determines the priorities of these backends in part by detecting the platform. The problem is, you can't know that the OS_X module is not needed until you import it and inquire about its viability... unless you write an external routine to filter these backends, which would supersede the existing mechanism for prioritizing backends, at which point you have code in at least two places and using different techniques to achieve the same thing. I don't think we want to go that route.

The unittest issue shouldn't be an issue, as the test suite will use (and implicitly requires) unittest2 to run.

The issue is worse than just the use of with statement, though. Most of the tests use class decorators to skip whole classes when the backend isn't supported. Class decorators are not supported until Python 2.6.

I started work backporting the whole test suite to run under Python 2.5, but found it really made the code ugly. Given that we've made it this far without tests on 2.5, I'm going to mark this as won't fix.

If someone feels strongly about supporting tests on Python 2.5, please prepare a pull request that updates the class decorators to simply wrap the class objects. Make sure the patch is clean (doesn't make other, unrelated changes), so it can be easily backed out at such a time that Python 2.5 is no longer supported.

* in Python <3.5, only 100 named groups are allowed: https://bugs.python.org/issue22437

I imagine it's a big speed hit and a bit of a code complexity hit to write Python code to try the regexes in succession instead of the pipe-joined large ones, but that's the first thing that comes to mind. With *jupyter-gmaps* 0.7.0, you can now integrate gmaps into widget-based applications. The following lets us draw lines on the map and print the distance of each line in a text box below the map:

```python
import ipywidgets as widgets
import geopy.distance
import geopy.format
import gmaps

API_KEY = "AIza..."
gmaps.configure(api_key=API_KEY)

class DistanceCalculculator(object):
    
    def __init__(self):
        self._figure = gmaps.figure()
        self._drawing = gmaps.drawing_layer(show_controls=False, mode='LINE')
        self._drawing.on_new_feature(self._new_feature_callback)
        self._figure.add_layer(self._drawing)
        self._start_box = widgets.Text(
            description='start: ',
            disabled=True,
            layout={'width': '30%', 'margin': '10px 0 0 0'}
        )
        self._end_box = widgets.Text(
            description='end: ',
            disabled=True,
            layout={'width': '30%', 'margin': '10px 0 0 0'}
        )
        self._distance_box = widgets.Text(
            description='distance: ',
            disabled=True,
            layout={'width': '30%', 'margin': '10px 0 0 0'}
        )
        self._geocoder = geopy.geocoders.GoogleV3(api_key=API_KEY)
        _info_container = widgets.HBox([self._start_box, self._end_box, self._distance_box])
        self._container = widgets.VBox([self._figure, _info_container])
        
    def _format_location(self, location):
        latitude, longitude = location
        fmt = '%(degrees)d%(deg)s° %(minutes)dm'
        latitude_str = geopy.format.format_degrees(latitude, fmt=fmt) + ' N' if abs(latitude) > 0.0 else ' S'
        longitude_str = geopy.format.format_degrees(longitude, fmt=fmt) + ' E' if abs(longitude) > 0.0 else ' W'
        return '{}, {}'.format(latitude_str, longitude_str)
        
    def _clear_info(self):
        self._distance_box.value = ''
        
    def _get_distance_km(self, start_location, end_location):
        return geopy.distance.great_circle(start_location, end_location).kilometers
        
    def _set_info(self, start_location, end_location):
        distance_km = self._get_distance_km(start_location, end_location)
        self._distance_box.value = '{:.2f} km'.format(distance_km)
        self._start_box.value = self._format_location(start_location)
        self._end_box.value = self._format_location(end_location)
        
    def _new_feature_callback(self, feature):
        try:
            start_location = feature.start
            end_location = feature.end
        except AttributeError:
            return # Not a line

        # Clear distance box to signify to the user that something is happening
        self._clear_info()

        # Remove all markers other than the one that has just been added.
        self._drawing.features = [feature]

        # Compute the address and display it
        self._set_info(start_location, end_location)
        
    def render(self):
        return self._container
    
DistanceCalculculator().render()
```

```
$ python -m timeit "
from pydash import filter_
x = [{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, {'a': 2, 'b': 3, 'c': 4}]
filter_(x, {'a': 1})"
10000 loops, best of 3: 29.6 usec per loop

$ python -m timeit "
x = [{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, {'a': 2, 'b': 3, 'c': 4}]
filter(lambda y: y['a'] is 1, x)"
1000000 loops, best of 3: 0.882 usec per loop
```

which brings the difference down to *~34x slower* than the native `filter`.

```
$ python -m timeit "
from pydash import filter_
x = [{'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}, {'a': 2, 'b': 3, 'c': 4}]
filter_(x, {'a': 1})"
10000 loops, best of 3: 49.4 usec per loop
```

which is *~56x slower* than the native `filter`.

A major difference I didn't see mentioned here is quamash only working for the main application event loop, i.e. QApplication's main/UI thread. Meanwhile the "let's put an event loop in an event loop so you can loop while you loop"-approach actually works for arbitrary QThreads. Sample code:

```py
class ...(QObject):
    @pyqtSlot()
    def run(self):
        # Connected to QThread.started; thus will be called in the new thread.
        asyncio.set_event_loop(asyncio.new_event_loop())
        self.evloop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self.evloop.run_until_complete(self.run_event_loop())

    async def run_event_loop(self):
        """
        Runs the event loop. Will only complete if the thread receives an interruption request
        (QThread.requestInterruption()).

        Since we are only using asyncio for coordination, the loop will block for Qt events.
        """
        thread = self.thread()
        while not thread.isInterruptionRequested():
            await asyncio.sleep(0)
            thread.eventDispatcher().processEvents(QEventLoop.AllEvents|QEventLoop.WaitForMoreEvents)
```

The sequence to get such a thread to join is thus as follows:

```py
thread.requestInteruption()  # Set interruption request flag
thread.eventDispatcher().wakeup()  # Causes processEvents() to return even if no real events arrive
thread.quit()  # Causes event loop to quit (the one running in QThread.run, not run_event_loop())
thread.join()  # Thread will exit now and can be joined.
```

find_in_structure and get_from_structure is an example of operating with paths, which there are many ways to do.  ("Path" meaning a sequence of attribute / item accesses which can be stored and applied to any object.)  One implementation I've made works with future objects -- a path would be stored expressing what data to access on the future response.

I'm hoping there is some clean abstraction that covers many cases.  Something like SQL for trees.  There are systems like JSONiq and XQuery (http://www.jsoniq.org/) which make a good reference point.  Their downsides are they require an external library and I think operating on "result sets" is the wrong abstraction.

There are some other examples out there too this is a fun research project for me for now :-)  Maybe something will come of it.

```python
import txtorcon
from cryptography.hazmat.backends import default_backend
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization
from twisted.internet.defer import inlineCallbacks
from twisted.internet.task import react

private_key = rsa.generate_private_key(
    public_exponent=65537,
    key_size=1024,
    backend=default_backend()
)

privkey = private_key.private_bytes(
   encoding=serialization.Encoding.PEM,
   format=serialization.PrivateFormat.TraditionalOpenSSL,
   encryption_algorithm=serialization.NoEncryption()
)

privkey = privkey.replace('-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----', '')
privkey = privkey.replace('-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----', '')
privkey = privkey.replace('\n', '')
privkey = 'RSA1024:' + privkey

@react
@inlineCallbacks
def main(reactor):
    tor = yield txtorcon.launch(reactor)
    hs = txtorcon.EphemeralHiddenService(["80 127.0.0.1:8082"], privkey)
    yield hs.add_to_tor(tor.protocol)
    print hs.hostname
    try:
        print hs.private_key
    except AttributeError:
        pass
    yield hs.remove_from_tor(tor.protocol)
```

A part of this issue has been fixed in the current txtorcon version - it does "persist" the hostname, because that is returned by the `ADD_ONION` command. The problem is that the private key is not (when you provide one).

The code persisting the private key in `txtorcon/torconfig.py` is:

```python
 464         if self._key_blob == 'NEW:BEST':
 465             self.private_key = ans['PrivateKey']
```

Suggestions:

- Shouldn't the conditional check the key starts with `'NEW'` (like `__init__` does) or being more strict, that it matches both `'NEW:BEST'` and `'NEW:RSA1024'`?
- In `__init__` it's also checked that the length of the key is correct, so maybe an `else` clause with `self.private_key = self._key_blob` would work.

What do you think?

Thank you for the report. I can reproduce this issue, but it's strange. For the moment, a workaround is to use `write('hello world', exact=False)`. This will send actual key events instead of typing the characters with OS-specific typing commands. It'll keep you from using characters not present in your keyboard, but fixes the double-letter issue.

Notes:

- this only happens on Windows.
- this only happens if using `exact=True` (which is the default option in Window), which relies on `_os_keyboard.type_unicode`.
- putting a delay between events fixes the problem, but the delay required is enormous (e.g. `write('hello world', delay=0.2)`), with larger and larger requires for more repeated characters in a row.

There is an `if argument is None` statement within `Logger`, that causes Jedi not to evaluate both branches. Because of that if statement, the output is different. This is actually very advanced type inference, but it makes Jedi look a bit stupid in this case. 

Look for example at this case:

```
def foo(bar=None):
    if bar is None:
        return 1
    else:
        return '1'
```

Here the type inference makes a lot of sense, because it returns different types. However in your case, it obviously doesn't. The problem is that Jedi's type inference is not good enough when the param is there. I hope that this improves, but until that point, this issue will just confuse people for a little bit.

~ Dave

I tried to investigate this problem since it happens to me as well. As it was ok for some imports but not in some others, I used following code to find out where exactly `full_name` method is raising errors:
```python
import jedi

s = jedi.Script('''import os
os.''', 2, len('os.'))

for item in s.completions():
    try:
        item.full_name
    except Exception as e:
        print(item.name, e)
```
And here is the result:
```bash
$ python test.py
ce not enough values to unpack (expected 1, got 0)
nt not enough values to unpack (expected 1, got 0)
_exit not enough values to unpack (expected 1, got 0)
_have_functions not enough values to unpack (expected 1, got 0)
```
So I tried to figure out what is going on in [Python os module](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/ea720fe7e99d68924deab38de955fe97f87e2b29/Lib/os.py) and it looks like these imports shouldn't happen if `ne` is not available in `sys.builtin_module_names`! So if we don't have the module, `infer_import` from `evaluates/imports.py` will send back an empty set and this will cause `module_context, = name.infer()` in `api/classes.py` to raise `ValueError`.  

For now, I've fixed this problem as you can see [in my fork](https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/compare/master...shahinism:bug/%23873?expand=1), but I'm not sure if this is the best way to cover this issue.

I think it's worth re-evaluating this. The recent intermediate certificate change has caused issues for a number of users who were using the defaults suggested by this project (such as the renewal script).

I'm all in favour of keeping this script as small as possible; there are plenty of clients who cater to users with other requirements, after all. But this is an essential feature: Certificates are practically useless without a certificate chain. Automation, which is basically the main goal of ACME, is basically impossible to deploy with `acme-tiny` because ACME servers make no guarantees whatsoever about issuer certificates staying the same for any period of time.

My proposal wouldn't change the current default behavior of `acme-tiny` and could likely be implemented in <= 10 LOC. I don't think it introduces any unnecessary complexity - it merely implements an integral part of the ACME specification.

Hi @rspeed @floreo,

It's a great idea to set a User-Agent for the client! The proposed UA is a solid improvement over no UA, but you might also want to consider the advice from [Section 5.1 of ACME Draft-04](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-04#section-5.1):
>  ACME clients SHOULD send a User-Agent header in accordance with
>  [RFC7231], including the name and version of the ACME software in
>  addition to the name and version of the underlying HTTP client
>  software.

I completely understand your desire to prevent exploitation of your work.

However, I don't think LGPL *or* GPL meets the requirements you've described. For a standalone tool, either would work, depending on how you want to draw the line for "using" SSLyze. If you want to prevent a proprietary tool from even using using sslyze as a library, then GPL works fine; but if you're fine with a proprietary tool that *uses* sslyze, but publishes any modifications they make to sslyze itself, then LGPL would be better.

However, neither LGPL *or* GPL prevents use of sslyze in a SaaS configuration. If I (or anyone else, for that matter) was to write a website that uses sslyze, then I won't be distributing a binary - and so, I won't trigger any of the clauses of the GPL that would also require distribution of my source code. 

That's what the AGPL is designed to achieve - to force anyone who writes a web service that uses an AGPL'd library to also release the source code for the entire web service. However, there's no "Lesser AGPL" to my knowledge, so there is no middle ground - I either have to release all the code for my web service, or not use your AGPL'd library. As you may expect, this pretty much kills *any* commercial use of AGPL'd software. Which might be your intent - and it's your software, so that's your prerogative.

So - lets put this in concrete terms. I'm working on a SaaS which hopes to offer, amongst many other things, verification of SSL configuration of web servers and certificates. My intention is to essentially use sslyze as-is - and any changes I made would be returned to sslyze itself (as I've already done with #200). 

By the strict letter of the law, there's nothing stopping me from using your code right now under the terms of the GPL, because the GPL doesn't cover the SaaS use case. But I'd rather adhere to the spirit of the law and honour your wishes. 

If I'm understanding your reply correctly, licensing terms notwithstanding, this is the sort of usage you're OK with. Have I misunderstood your intent?

> the typical case is that c << a,b,

My point is that the opening angle, theta, is typically in arcmin, so sin(theta) and tan(theta) are << 1.  You're right that if the source is at a much higher redshift than the lens, c could be significant compared to a,b.  But it will still be the case that b ~= a+c.  So the (a+c-b) term is small, and if done naively can end up with much lower numerical precision than you might want.

I have a different approach (using a x b) that should be more accurate.

> Is this Rperp or Rlens? 

L is Rlens in Tom's equation.  And in mine above that.  Rperp is defined as c^2 - (b-a)^2 using the same diagram.

> I think the Arc metric corresponds to the chord that Mike mentions,

No.  Arc doesn't exist yet.  But it will be a great circle distance, rather than a chord distance.  The distinction doesn't matter much for smallish angles, but at large separations, people might want to use the actual great circles for their distance.  cf. the discussion on issue #39.

I've been looking on the overall content of the package, specifically the parser and I would like to see what you think about the following suggestions:

- Extend `HeaderItem` to accept extra items to comply with the LAS 3.0 standard. 
Your current design is this:
```python
 HeaderItem = namedlist("HeaderItem", ["mnemonic", "unit", "value", "descr"])
```
What I'm proposing is to include two extra items, `"format"` and `"association"`:
```python
 HeaderItem = namedlist("HeaderItem", ["mnemonic", "unit", "value", "description", "format", "association"])
```
Those items would be empty in case `"VERS"` is `1.2` or `2.0`. I also think this would ease the support of `datetime` objects as suggested in #1.

- Drop the `"curves"` in favor of `"definition"` and keep a consistency with the `3.0` new naming convention: 

> ~Section_Parameter, ~Section_Definition, and ~Section_Data

It would be some sort of always treat as `3.0` first approach...

Anyway, just my two cents to align my thoughts before doing some actual work.

@kislyuk awesome, that's a great help, thank you! I'll try the `logging.shutdown()` on the handlers. If it helps, most of our scripts run within a `while True` loop, watching for messages to process, and we just kill them with a `SIGINT` signal.

I haven't figured out if there are only specific processes that don't log all messages, or conversely some processes that always log _all_ messages, but from what I've seen, it seems like every process drops messages here and there. There are some processes that have something like this to do additional cleanup on exit:

```
def signal_term_handler(signal, frame):
    # do some additional cleanup here
    sys.exit(0)
signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, signal_term_handler)
```

I'm somewhat new to Python (coming primarily from Ruby and JS), so I'm not sure about the intricacies of Python's logging or signal handling, so I don't know if the above simply adds functionality (as the name "handler" would imply), or if it overwrites the standard signal handling completely, such that you must manually include the operations that the standard signal handling would normally perform, such as `logging.shutdown()`. I would hope it's the former, but figured I'd mention it in case it's related.

And finally, some of our processes happen within machines that get terminated, and I'm not positive we always explicitly kill the process before terminating the machine. However, I've seen this behavior of not logging all messages even on my local machine when I am killing the Python process (and not shutting down my machine or closing the terminal), so while it could be also related, it doesn't seem to be the sole cause at least.

Anyway, figured I'd include all this info here in case it helps someone else that comes across this, but seems like it's (probably... maybe?) not specifically a Watchtower issue, but more possibly a Python logging issue, so I appreciate your feedback and direction.

I think what I'll try doing is calling `logging.shutdown()` in our exit handlers (and create one for our processes that don't have one), and make sure that our machine-terminating scripts always kill the processes first before destroying the machines, and see if that helps.

This library is basically a wrapper around the Arlo API used by their web app (and mobile app).
This library lets you do almost everything you can do from the web app UI.

It works by connecting to the arlo webserver through their API. I tend to ask as little info as possible for people to sign up.

To do this, I present them with the same login form.

- Have required fields in each form, so that a user must fill those fields in before they can continue to use the website.

Time (HH:MM:SS) | Previous Volume | Current Volume | Ratio
------------ | ------------- | ------------- | ------------- |

... | | |
04:00:00 | 300 | 300 | 1  

Our line would hold the ratio values for every minute. In a trading day, there's 6.5 hours * 60 minutes = 390 minutes. So for 1 minute charts you would need a period of 390 in order to 'align' both days.

Maybe something like this:

class RelativeVolume(bt.Indicator):

The other thing is the period=390 is hard coded to the 1 minute time frame. A time frame of 1 second will yield 23400 candles. At runtime, can the indicator have knowledge of what time frame is being used? Something like:

The `tick` data, from for example *Oanda* can be resampled to any larger timeframe. This was added in October 2015

  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2015-09-25-tickdata-resample/resample-tickdata/

The issue is not if the data is `tick` data but in which format is presented. Many times this is a pair of *bid/ask* prices. Following a discussion in a issue a blog post came out as to how to load *bid/ask* data directly into *backtrader* using the `GenericCSVData`.

  - http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2016-04-14-bidask-data-to-ohlc/bidask-data-to-ohlc/

The sample chooses the *bid* field as the price for the `tick` by telling the `GenericCSVData` sample that `open`, `high`, `low` and `close` can all be found at position `5`.

In this sample the *ticks* are simply *compressed*, but nothing prevents to give the *cerebro.resampledata* a `timeframe` argument to resample the *ticks* to a larger timeframe

For `range` bars there has been nothing. Although `backtrader` tries to be as time agnostic as possible, things happen with a *timestamp* and some parts do always expect a `datetime` field. This is possibly not a large obstacle to implement *range* bars, but it has to be looked into with some care.

The new approach is much more predictable.

usage: /Users/mhi/bin/nvr [arguments]

Helper tool for nvim that provides --remote and friends.

    $ nvr --remote-silent foo bar quux
    $ nvr foo bar quux

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -l                    change to previous window via ":wincmd p"

It has been my experience that CAs have been responding if a certificate is revoked well after the certificate itself has expired. My application is validating code signed certificates where the common case is the file was signed in 2012 with certificate valid from 2011 - 2013 and then countersigned/timestamped in 2012 with a certificate that is valid until 2020.

In this case the moment I'm validating the signing certificate chain is from 2012 because that is when the counter signature took place. All certificates on that chain need to be valid in 2012 and not currently revoked (2016). 

When I validate the associated counter signing chain the moment is NOW. This may only be the case for code signing, but anything that is counter signed the entire chain should be checked for validation so I don't know how a CA would get away with forgetting about keys that have been compromised just because they are expired. Especially if they have a key usage that includes things like document signatures.
I have added support for extra claims in the 1.1 release.

Could you tell me what operating system you are on?
Could you also run the following commands in a Python shell with an environment that has `python-dateutil` installed? Let me know what gets returned please:
```python
In [23]: import dateutil
In [24]: dateutil.tz.gettz('IRST')
```

### breakdown
Yes, the problem is the test and probably the assumption that the operating system the test is running on doesn't have zoneinfo for the [timezone abbreviation -- IRST](http://www.timeanddate.com/time/zones/irst). Good catch!

`dateutil.tz.gettz` is basically a big `if/else` booby trap hunting for the right timezone files ( [here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L932-L937) ). On Ubuntu 14.04 it is looking to resolve `IRST` at this location:
```python
In [22]: ls -lah /usr/share/zoneinfo/I*
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1.2K Jun 13  2014 /usr/share/zoneinfo/Iceland
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1.7K Jun 13  2014 /usr/share/zoneinfo/Iran
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 2.2K Jun 13  2014 /usr/share/zoneinfo/Israel
```
It doesn't find it and therefore tries to replace the tzinfo with `None` which mean it returns a naive datetime ( [here](https://github.com/akoumjian/datefinder/blob/master/datefinder.py#L185-L186) ) as the test was designed.

My guess is that the `dateutil.tz.gettz` lookup resolves for you and returns legitimate tzinfo. 

The test should probably use a really really wrong timezone abbreviation such as `NOT_A_TIMEZONE` ;-)

I would not adjust `excel_time` after the fact, except to compensate for the 1900 leap year bug.

XlsxWriter cannot know how an incoming Python datetime was created, nor should it care. If the function is receiving a Python datetime, then it really should honor the date portion, no matter what it is (as long as it is representable in Excel; and if it's not, then raise an exception). There is no dilemma or compromise here. If the caller is passing in a datetime when they really should be passing in a time (or a date portion of 1899-12-31), well that's on them, and they have the power to fix it very easily. XlsxWriter should not be presuming to know the caller's intent better than the caller does, especially when doing so *removes the ability* to represent a valid value that normally would be representable in Excel.

It's different when the function is receiving a Python time, because then there is no Python date portion to preserve. In this case, it's reasonable for XlsxWriter to do its best to translate that to Excel's idiom.

this is the relevant XML snippet:

```cpp
...
      <memberdef kind="function" id="namespaceFoo_1a438de3b4c677344081baef1ec352f691" prot="public" static="no" const="no" explicit="no" inline="no" virt="non-virtual">

        <param>
          <type>int</type>
          <declname>a</declname>
        </param>
        <param>
          <type>std::string</type>
          <declname>b</declname>
        </param>
        <param>
          <type>char(&)</type>
          <declname>things</declname>
          <array>[5]</array>
        </param>
        <param>
          <type>bool</type>
        </param>
        <param>
          <type>char(&)</type>
          <array>[42]</array>
        </param>
        <param>

          <array>[]</array>
        </param>

          <type>double</type>
          <array>[1337]</array>
        </param>
...
```

Yeah, this is a known limitation. See the note that starts with "at least two things make it a bit awkward to replicate a 'menuconfig'-like interface..." in the README. Kconfiglib always models the relations exactly as described in the Kconfig files, so it's consistent in that way. It's just that menuconfig adds some "sugar" on top of that in the form of implicit menus.

I think the proper API would be to either add a flag to `get_parent()` to (possibly) get an "implicit" parent, or to add a separate method. Kconfiglib's API could do with less messiness though...

The empirically best number of merge operations likely depends on your dataset and language pair. We have some numbers by @bhaddow on WMT 2018 datasets for EN<->CS,ET,FI, and BLEU differences between 30,000 and 90,000 merge operations are small (although the effect on rare words might be larger), so 30,000 is a good starting point. If your dataset is relatively small (less than 1 million sentence pairs), I'd recommend that you also try out a smaller number of merge operations, since the model is unlikely to learn a useful representation of a subword that only occurs a few times.

If you have related languages, it is preferable to learn BPE units jointly on the source and target language (see https://github.com/rsennrich/subword-nmt#best-practice-advice-for-byte-pair-encoding-in-nmt ). For language pairs such as English-Chinese, there is no need that the number of operations be the same.

It ended up turning out that the issue was django_seo_js is passing on a `Content-Encoding` header from PrerenderIO, which is causing all the problems.

Subclassing with the below code fixed it:

```python
from django.http import HttpResponse
from django_seo_js.backends import PrerenderIO
from django_seo_js.backends.base import RequestsBasedBackend, IGNORED_HEADERS
from fabric.colors import *

IGNORED_HEADERS = set(IGNORED_HEADERS) | {'Content-Encoding'}

class FixedRequestsBasedBackend(RequestsBasedBackend):

    def build_django_response_from_requests_response(self, response):
        # Key difference -- we're excluding "content-encoding" from the response
        r = HttpResponse(response.content)
        for k, v in response.headers.items():
            if k not in IGNORED_HEADERS:
                r[k] = v
        r['content-length'] = len(response.content)
        r.status_code = response.status_code
        return r


class FixedPrerenderIO(FixedRequestsBasedBackend, PrerenderIO):
    pass
```

@ilevkivskyi, PEP 8 specifically makes an exception for keyword arguments and default values. It's a few bullets down under "Other Recommendations":

> Don't use spaces around the = sign when used to indicate a keyword argument or a default parameter value.
>
> Yes:
>
> ```def complex(real, imag=0.0):
    return magic(r=real, i=imag)```
>
> No:
>
> ```def complex(real, imag = 0.0):
    return magic(r = real, i = imag)```

Here's my opinion after spending some time with the library:

 - The current way of doing things is IMHO too complicated. We are pretending to be file objects but we don't have to be! Streaming JSON, in it's very core is just that- a stream. In Python we just give it a different name- generator.
 - The way I'd implement it is just having a very thin layer over file objects:

```python
def read_stream(f):
    for line in f:
        yield json.loads(line)

def get_emitter(f, sep='\n'):
    def emitter(obj):
        f.write(json.dumps(obj) + sep))
    return emitter
```

 - Usage:
```python
emit = get_emitter(open('file', 'w'))
emit({"key": "value"})
emit({"key": "value"})
list(read_stream(open('file', 'r'))) == [{"key": "value"}] * 2
```
 - Since we need to have an option to store the errors I'd also imagine that you want to write a simple class to store the state as well. An alternative, though it tends to get less used in Python is to have an error callback. Just call the supplied function every time we get an error.
 - You can also write a convenience function over the `read_stream` primitive:

```python
def consume(f, **options):
    if isinstance(f, str):
        f = open(stream, mode='r')
    return read_stream(f, **options)
```

@amorphic It would be nice to have that symmetry between jsonrpc and xmlrpc in python 3. I have a few ideas that might help achieve this.

1. I believe you can put multiple packages on pypi that deliver the same importable package name (obviously installing multiple would cause issues). Only the name used on pypi (thus by pip) needs to be unique.
1. You can have multiple root import packages in a single pypi package. So json-rpc package could deliver both a jsonrpclib importable package and a jsonrpc(.client/server) importable package in one bundle and share implementation. The entry points (jsonrpclib and jsonrpc) could either support both python 2 & 3 or assert that they are only loaded on the respective version (if it saves work...).
1. If having the original old jsonrpc package floating around on pypi or github causes you other issues, you could reach out to the documented owners to negotiate for custody of their project / package if they're not interested in it anymore. If this fails, I've heard it's possible to reach out to pypi, they may have a process for reassigning authority over a package that's important to the community where the owners are unresponsive / unhelpful. Github may also be sympathetic to such things and (again unconfirmed) may be able to help funnel users to a more relevant fork of an old project.

Just some thoughts...

If the `app_label` attribute of the AppConfig instance is not defined, it is created using the last component of the `name` attribute, which in this case, is turns out to be the string `'default'`. Those `np.ascontiguousarray` calls are really quite expensive.  What I can try then is only conditionally applying those calls by checking `x.flags.c_continguous` first.  The vast majority of NumPy arrays are continguous.  Similarly we don't want to cast.  NumPy assumes you want a copy in those calls and you're not modifying the input array, so if it's not needed, it should be skipped.

Also we could explicitly create `npy_float32` and `npy_float64` branches. The easiest way to do this would be to have two copies of each function.  I suspect that's not too much maintenance overhead?  And then map everything from `npy_int32` to `npy_float64` and all the smaller dtypes to `npy_float32`.

As extra note on the explanation above:

- If you work with multiple developers on a project, they each have to enable the interoperability mode and create keys which then have to be picked up by you project config/settings. This means quite some unnecessary work and config. Alternatively you would have to distribute the credentials for 1 account to everybody. This is not wanted.
- When deploying on Google Cloud instances or (kubernetes) clusters, there are by default application credentials present for authentication against GCS (service accounts). It is these services accounts that are used to manage roles and authentication on the Google Cloud platform everywhere. On developer machines, they are also present system-wide for all applications to pick up by default. Each program thus runs under the dev account when running locally, and uses the instance/cluster's account when deployed somewhere, and this all happens automatically. They are the recommended way of authenticating and are used throughout the platform (https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/application-default-credentials), available for multiple languages.

> My reading then is that the filterset reads the full get request and doesn't rely on any tweaks in URLconf.

that is correct. it uses querystrings (the thing after `?` in the uri) which are within the domain of the view, not the urlconf.

just a note though that urlfilter is meant for filtering within a particular model. from your quick example you should prolly filter the `model` parameter itself in the `get_queryset` and then let django url filter filter within that. in other words, use django ORM to filter url kwargs and let urlfilter filter querystrings.

```python
class MyListView(ListView):
     def get_queryset(self):
         qs = super(MyListView, self).get_queryset()
         qs = qs.filter(model=self.kwargs['model'])
         fs = UserFilterSet(data=self.request.GET, queryset=qs)
         return fs.filter()
````

that will allow to do things like:

```
/list/foo/  # all foo results
/list/foo/?created_timestamp__gt=2017-01-01
```

the idea is that the model regex itself should validate the `model` value hence no need for django url filter (e.g. in `(?P<model>\w+)`).

Per looking at the ```csrf_protect``` token in a cookie instead of the headers, that actually would not work. The cookies are always going to be sent to the server, regardless of if the request comes from your site, or if it comes from a malicious site attempting to do a CSRF attack. If we check for the token in the cookies (which, it should be there), we are not actually preventing CSRF attacks at all.

In order to prevent them, we are relying on the header having the CSRF protection token, and the only way the header should be able to get the token is if the person making the request can use javascript on the given domain to access that CSRF token from the cookie, which is what actually provides the CSRF protection.

This article has a good write up of these ideas: http://www.redotheweb.com/2015/11/09/api-security.html. If you wanted more information or clarification, you can also hit me up and I would be more then happy to go over this with you.

If you want to revert the csrf changes, and update the pull request message, I would be happy to merge this. Thanks for contributing! 👍

personally I wouldn't do that. Redis is not intended to keep persisted data over long periods. Keeping a bucket in Redis for a short period is ok, so that a reconnecting client can use that, but for long term persistence I always would use the means Django provides. It also is much cheaper and safer to store data in the database rather than in volatile memory.

Yes, I know, you may argue that Redis persists is data on shutdown, but that's only true for clean shutdowns, not for exlectricity outages etc.

Django moved to a migrations infrastructure from version 1.7, which deprecated the need for South.

FWIW, migrations are easier and more powerful than South, and have been written by the author of South - in fact his work means he's now a core contributor to the Django code base.  

So the error you are seeing is unlikely to be related to Zinnia - I've installed this app in a new Django 1.8 installation and got no South errors to my knowledge.

I would recommend removing South from your installed apps.

The [Django docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/) have [a section dedicated to the new migrations](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/migrations/) which include a part on [upgrading from South](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/topics/migrations/#upgrading-from-south)

I'm not sure we're looking at the same README, but you haven't explained why specifically fatomic isn't sufficient for your needs. Fatomic takes care of all of them, as os.replace/os.rename can both handle existent target and error handling is handled just as well as you do. The reason why I don't use fatomic/activestate is because they don't handle src/dst directory synchronization and so I'm not sure the file has made it to disk after leaving the context manager. The activestate solution also doesn't do a good job on providing a good win32 fallback for older python versions.

Maybe I have been misunderstood, I'm not advocating for the removal of ctypes, I don't even think there is a Python2/3 self contained cross platform solution without them. I'm specifically advocating for the use of os.replace whenever possible so as to comply with current python documentation. It is worth noting that MoveFileExW is not guaranteed to be atomic as per a few sources on the web. So if we have to choose between a dubious win32 api and a modern module that the documentation states is atomic and cross platform, I would think the discussion is over. The right choice seems to be pretty obvious.

It's really no big deal for me to add `git+https://github.com/dhulke/python-atomicwrites.git#egg=atomicwrites` to my requirements.txt and fix this on my own repository. The reason why I'm arguing here is just so I'm 100% sure I'm not missing anything else like I did on my first PR. So if you're telling me you don't want to default to os.replace just because it'll add an additional codepath and the code wont look prettier and you'd rather have dubious but prettier code than to have a solid and "by the book" but longer code, then that would be enough for me and I would just use my own repository.

It looks like you're importing the `model` field instead of the `form` field. Wherever you're importing `PhoneNumberField` for your form, verify that it is importing from `formfields`:

```
from phonenumber_field.formfields import PhoneNumberField
```

The reason you were getting this error is that `widget`s are part of form fields in Django. The form `PhoneNumberField` is based on `CharField`. The `modelfield`, which I believe you are currently importing, is for use on models and would not have a `widget` attribute.

@nextghost 'cause pyDAL is not web2py, and you can use it in different ways rather than instantiate the whole DAL on every request. pyDAL is used by other frameworks that use pre-forking and the ability to use it in a multi-threaded environment is a feature. Check this scheme:

```
- main process
  - static POOLS
  - DAL instance/adapter instance
    - thread1
      - connection 1
        - cursor 1
        - cursor 2
      - connection 2
        - cursor 1
    - thread 2
      - connection 1
        - cursor 1
      - connection 2
        - cursor 1
        - cursor 2
```

if every thread performs it's own `connect`, your `after_reconnect` is performed 4 times. That should be correct if the contents of `after_reconnect` should be performed on every reconnection, but the configuration of the adapter (json stuff on postgres) should be performed just one time on the adapter, independently on how many times you re-use a connection or if a connection fails. @milanvdm  Thanks for taking a look!

I'm not _100%_ convinced myself either since I haven't run the code with CeleryExecutor :P but I am hopeful that this could potentially solve the issue in the distributed setup as well. Reason being:

When `clear` is called via the UI, the webserver will set the state of the LocalTaskJob to `shutdown`, this information is stored in the jobs table in the airflow metadata db.

The LocalTaskJob (which is created via `airflow run --local` command and is running in one of the workers) will heartbeat periodically, each time it will read from the db and check its own state , and once it detects that it needs to be shut down, it will attempt to "kill" itself, which will call the on_kill method that eventually sends a SIGTERM signal to the raw process that's doing the work...

Would be great to have someone running CeleryExecutor or have experience with Celery to test this out. I'll see what I can do on my end as well.

1. Why would you need to change all operator code (airflow.operator.*) to change its logger? It will inherit it from TaskInstance (I think), if not you could use "propagate = True" in the logging config and set "airflow.operators". Or add an extra method called something like "setTaskInstance" to TaskHandler, as they operators will inherit the parent logger. Obviously we need to try this out. I wouldn't necessarily call it critical as these kind of changes could be acceptable.

2. I think you would need to grab the template from the configuration and not do it in TaskHandler 'fixed'. Arguments from the config are password as keyword arguments to the constructor of the Handler. https://docs.python.org/2/library/logging.config.html#logging-config-dictschema

3. I don't think that really matters. You can just iterate over logging.handlers and see if one exposes the ReadMixin (or read method) and you are fine: they are logging all the same information. You could also make this a configuration item in airflow.cfg, i.e. Handler to use to read the logs.

I would use the flush method for uploading (https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/2.7/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L770), but that's arguable.

I think you don't you need to close the handlers by hand, I presume these are closed on interpreter shutdown. But that can be tested of course.

By default the hashes are computed on demand (and using multiple cores), but you can enable some options to heuristically compute the hash as file change.

https://github.com/facebook/watchman/commit/802d37abe570fef81547cac3d346eef69b3ec93b is the commit that added those options.

So you'd stick something like this in a file named `.watchmanconfig` in the watched root to enable it:

```
{
   "content_hash_warming": true
}
```

there are also some options to control the size of the cache (`content_hash_max_items`.  This defaults to 128k.  You want this to be set > the number of files in your tree, otherwise the cache will thrash), the maximum number of items that will be warmed up per settle (`content_hash_max_warm_per_settle`; default 1024.  You may want this to be larger if you have a lot of files changing all at once), and whether to defer settling until after the warm up has completed (`content_hash_warm_wait_before_settle`; this is mostly useful if you have a live subscription).

The term `settle` here refers to a brief idle period after receiving a burst of change notifications from the OS.  The default settings, if warming is enabled, mean that up to 1024 files will get hashed at each settle out.  If you have more files than this changing all at once you'll want to increase this setting to ensure that the hashes are computed in time for your next query.

If you want to support Py2 then you'd have to construct your traitlets ahead of time and then dynamically add them to the body of the class, ala:

```
_myclass_traits = [('dict_trait', Dict(...)), ('list_trait', List(...))]

class MyTraitedClass(HasTraits):
    _trait_order = []
    ls = locals()
    for name, value in _myclass_traits:
        ls[name] = value
        _trait_order.append(value)

# MyTraitedClass now has all the desired traits, plus a list of them in order as a class-level attribute named _trait_order.
```
This **might** be a reasonable thing to do if you're defining all your traited classes ahead of time, and you don't expect users to have to extend your traited classes.

If you're expecting your users to be participants in the class hierarchy I expect that it wouldn't be with the added confusion and complexity.

I actually do have some thoughts on that, but my thoughts would require changing the interface for get_credentials and create_credential_resolver. I think it'd be backward compatible. My basic idea is to add a named arg for a dict that contains all the args used with all the providers. Then, pass that dict and the session (as named args) to all providers during initialization. Then, I would be able to call get_credentials with the session and that dict to pass their values into a new credential resolver for each level of resolution. It would probably be good to have some way to prevent loops in the resolution by keeping a set of already resolved names and passing those in as well. The create_credential_resolver and get_credentials function would end up with this definition:

```
def create_credential_resolver(
        session, provider_config=None,  **kwargs):
    if provider_config is None:
        provider_config = set()
    if 'profile_name' in kwargs:
       profile_name = session.get_config_variable('profile') or 'default'
    ...and so on

def get_credential(
        session, provider_config=None, **kwargs):
    ...
```

Then I could pass the values into the get_credentials for any providers that needed to do a recursive resolution instead of doing something like constructing a new AssumeRoleProvider explicitly like I do in  my implementation of the recursive resolution now.

Having said all this, I feel like this change is a step between here and there. I think it's going to take a lot of fiddling to get the new interface right considering that create_credential_resolver current resolves a lot of magic variables. I think that logic may need to be refactored. I'd like to see this change go in as it demonstrably improves the status quo by allowing recursive role assumption when the leaf identity is an AWS user. I think this is a clear improvement that doesn't impede a more general solution like I described above, and I'd hate to gate this functionality on that functionality.

Thanks for taking a stab at this. Sadly due to design mistakes we've made in Mopidy's APIs I don't think we can merge this, but I can offer an alternative. But firstly, lets quickly go over the mistakes blocking this:

1) Since we don't dedupe the model info you'll be adding the image data once per track for every track in an album with the same image.
2) We return fat models in a ton a places we shouldn't, search being the biggest offender.

Additionally there are also the `browse` and `lookup` code paths which I didn't see in this PR.

As for the alternative, luckily for you 0.20 which should get released any day now has https://docs.mopidy.com/en/develop/api/backends/#mopidy.backend.LibraryProvider.get_images which works around a lot of these problems. So any change concerning album art should be using this API.

For this this you have two approaches, do something like https://gist.github.com/adamcik/786643627eeae5620629 which uses pyspotify (but I'm unclear on the libspotify license with respect to cover art use), over even better cleanup https://gist.github.com/adamcik/a0b72ec356264d06aaf6 which uses the spotify web APIs.

So if you were to clean up the web based one, maybe add some tests and then plug that into `get_images` I think we have a winner which I would support merging :-)

This is a known issue. The reason for the behavior is this:

- The remote interface is build on top of the dynamic library API.
- Originally the dynamic library API method `run_keyword` only accepted two arguments `name` (name of the keyword to execute) and `args` (arguments to that keyword).
- When the named argument syntax was introduced to Robot (i.e. being able pass arguments like `name=value`) we still needed to map those named arguments into positional arguments. With arguments that were not used we need to use their default values.
- When dynamic libraries tell what arguments certain keywords accept (`get_keyword_arguments` method), all information is just strings without any information about original default value types.

In practice the above means that, with your example, when you use `c=xxx`, we need to pass also all the arguments before `c`. The arguments are `a=None, b=2`, but because type information is lost the `args` passed to `run_keywords` is going to be `['None', '2', 'xxx']`.

This is obviously annoying and affected also SeleniumLibrary when it switched to the dynamic API (robotframework/SeleniumLibrary#843). With SL we couldn't wait for possible fixes in the dynamic API and decided to workaround the issue with `is_noney` utility that considers also string 'NONE' (case-insensitively) to be `None`. Numbers we already handled with `int()` and `float()`. The benefit of this workaround is that users can use the keyword in the data like `| Foo | a=2 | b=None |` and the keyword will still work correctly. Without explicit conversion both `2` and `None` would be strings.

Although there is a workaround, it would be better to fix the underlying problem. Luckily that ought to be pretty easy. In order to support free `**kwargs`, the `run_keyword` dynamic method has optionally supported three arguments `name`, `args`, `kwargs` since RF 2.8.2 (robotframework/robotframework#1500). We could change the dynamic API to pass also named arguments via `kwargs` making it possible to omit arguments that weren't used altogether. With the `c=xxx` example this would result `args` being empty and `kwargs` being `{'c': 'xxx'}`. As a result the actual method would not get any value for `a` or `b` (i.e. they'd get their default values). This change ought to be pretty simple but all changes like that are potentially backwards incompatible. In my opinion it could be done in RF 3.1. The change should then also be tested with the remote interface.

We can accept this as is as far as I am concerned just to get it working now. That said - @ScottWales, are you feeling frisky, because you could try to use `urlparse` in the fix to see how it goes.

Python 2:
```
import urlparse
urlparse.urljoin(base, catalog_ref)
```

Python 3:
```
import urllib.parse
urllib.parse.urljoin(base, catalog_ref)
```

Note that `catalog_ref` can be a relative path, or a fully qualified path. If relative, it is joined to `base`; if fully qualified, it is used as is. So a bonus of using `urlparse` is that it will  "do the right thing" under the hood if the for relative and fully qualified catalogRefs.

Hi, the shell detection went through a couple of changes in recent versions. The current logic works like this:

1. Check if `PIPENV_SHELL` is set—use it if available.
2. Detect the current shell automatically.
3. If the detection fails, use `SHELL` or `PYENV_SHELL`.
4. If either of the above two is set, use `COMSPEC` (added in master, will be in the next version).
5. Give up

So setting `SHELL` is less useful in the current version. We encourage users to try to rely on auto shell detection, and set `PIPENV_SHELL` if that fails. Could you expand the section to reflect this behaviour better, so it can be more useful to more people? A link to the [environment variable section](https://docs.pipenv.org/advanced/#configuration-with-environment-variables) would also be awesome.

@mtdeguzis I think there's evidence of a corrupted install in your traceback. Notice how `pkg_resources` is in `C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\pkg_resources` but `setuptools` is found at `build\bdist.win-amd64\egg\setuptools` (presumably installed as a .egg).

So you probably have two installs of setuptools, one as an egg and another 'externally managed' (maybe pip or virtualenv installed).

If you're using buildout or any installer than pip, your best bet is to work with that installer project to help ascertain where things went awry.

Most likely, you can work around the issue by removing the offending packages (i.e. `setuptools` and `pkg_resources` directories in site-packages), especially if you have a suitable setuptools*.egg there.

Based on this new evidence, I don't believe there's a flaw in Setuptools that needs to be corrected, but rather we're seeing evidence of improper installs that's only been exaggerated by the introduction of this (proper) import.

Feel free to continue to investigate. If you can find a valid scenario that I can replicate, especially without involving external installers like buildout, I can continue to investigate. If you can replicate the issue with buildout only, I suggest working with that project to isolate the issue.

I'm not entirely sure which one you mean, so I've provided 2 possible answers.

Passing Args to Klein Obj
----------------------------------
You can pass arguments into the ``Klein`` obj and then expose the underlaying Twisted ``Resource``.  For example, let's say you overloaded a Klein obj, you could then pass args into that object :
```
# twistdPlugin.py
app = OverloadedKlein('arg', 'arg', 'arg')

@app.route('/')
def home(r):
    return 'hello'

resource = app.resource
```

Then just run the ``twistd`` script as usual.


Passing Args via CLI
----------------------------
```
# Example:
$> twistd -n web --class=twistdPlugin.resource --arg1 --arg2 --arg3
```

This is a bigger undertaking (but not by much).  You basically have to write a ``twistd plugin``.  I'll provide some Stackoverflow questions so that you have some basic understanding.  Plugins are less intimidating than they sound and it doesn't take too much effort.

- http://stackoverflow.com/questions/15098827/when-launching-a-twisted-application-via-twistd-how-to-send-command-line-argume
- http://stackoverflow.com/questions/18244050/twistd-using-usage-options-in-a-tac-file
- https://twistedmatrix.com/documents/current/core/howto/tap.html

Is `(y, x)` the way to go here?

I assumed that for the end-user interface we use `(x, y)` order where we use pixel coordinate tuples and can't avoid the ambiguity by using `Table` objects.
The aperture classes (see e.g. [CircularAperture](http://photutils.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/photutils.aperture_core.CircularAperture.html)) use `positions=(x, y)` tuples as input).

I know `(y, x)` has the advantage that one can directly use it to index into numpy arrays, so I'm not saying I'm strongly pro `(x, y)`, but I think it should be carefully considered which is better.

Maybe at least always using [namedtuple](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#collections.namedtuple) and encouraging users to write code that indexes `x` and `y` by name instead of by index would help?

@mhvk as Numpy ufunc expert and proponent (see e.g. [this discussion](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/astropy-dev/0xYsvGl2AGs)) - maybe you could chime in here as well and make a suggestion how you think this new `astropy.healpix` should be written?

As for me: I'm reading the HEALPix paper and the code in this repo at the moment and trying to understand the details. It's not clear to me yet why we have 4000 lines of code in [cextern/astrometry.net](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/tree/master/cextern/astrometry.net) (plus another 200 in [interpolation.c](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/master/astropy_healpix/interpolation.c) and 500 lines of wrapper code in [astropy_healpix/core_cython.pyx](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-healpix/blob/master/astropy_healpix/core_cython.pyx).

Compare this to the license-compatible https://github.com/michitaro/healpix/blob/master/src/index.ts or https://github.com/ziotom78/Healpix.jl/blob/master/src/Healpix.jl that have ~ 500 lines of code and have very similar functionality (except interpolation missing) and could also serve as basis for a rewrite. I'm not saying we should do that, I'm just saying that we could consider a rewrite in whatever form we consider the best long-term solution for `astropy.healpix`. Given that we have tests and an API, a port of 500 or 1000 lines from another similar language should only be a day or two of work.

Some concrete questions:
- I think a pure Python & Numpy solution clearly not possible, because some functions require loops and can't be done as Numpy extressions?
- Numba is not an option yet for Astropy core yet, because of the heavy runtime dependency, right?
- If Numpy only or Numba aren't an option, that would always leave us with a solution involving Cython, right?
- Would a rewrite in Cython only bring benefits, or would you prefer a C library with Cython wrapper like we have now?
- Ufuncs or no ufuncs?

All (also @dstndstn): please comment and make a recommendation here, so that we can move `astropy.healpix` forward.
