Hey @danielballan, nice to see you again! =P

> correctly propagating metadata through, and updating it accurately to reflect the action of a given function, is a hard problem

Yes, but I will need to solve it at some point. So the question is whether it should live in scikit-image or in various field-specific subprojects. Over on the corresponding [imageio issue](https://github.com/imageio/imageio/issues/362#issuecomment-414624397), I kind of argued myself into the latter corner. =P A big advantage of field-specific option is that one only needs to worry about some subset of metadata. For example, in light microscopy, one wants emission wavelengths for each channel, but these would be pretty strange to deal with in normal photographs.

> the suggestion of capturing the "processing history" as part of this worries me.

Fair points. Just an idea. =) I don't necessarily mean to do something fancy. From the scikit-image perspective it could contain a list of strings containing the function name and the values of the arguments. Each function would just append itself to that list. (ie this would be intended for human consumption, not machine consumption. That's an easier problem I think.)

> would it make sense for the reading functions to return a image, dict_of_metadata, adopting canonical skimage names in the process

Yes, although as I mentioned on imageio, this is probably something to do at that level, rather than skimage.

> If a function accepts a dictionary of metadata, how do I know which keys in that dictionary affect the function's behavior and which are just being passed through (and maybe updated)?

I think @stefanv's distinction is very good here: let's *never* alter a function's behaviour based on `meta`. Rather, pass `meta` through and update if necessary, and maybe warn if a kwarg contradicts a meta parameter (such as `spacing=`, for example). 

> To teach users about metadata mangement, we can point them to other libraries we find useful.

This is a very good idea to grow from. I think if we can get imageio "standard metadata" working for a few image file types, then we can start to implement metadata handling not in code, but in documentation. As we write more docs about how to deal with metadata correctly, the right approach for code might become apparent.

> I don't see a strong case for implicit type conversion.

I'm coming along to this point of view. =)

@breznak pointed out an issue with the proposed solution to random seeds [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/pull/629/files/babf3890934f127a93fd4ad11c8794e98446ab76#r57984102). Here are a few options:

1. Change the Random class to treat `0` as a fixed seed and have a different value (accessed via final static variable, probably set to the max UInt value) that results in a random seed. This way we can stick with current plan without changing the signature of Random.
2. Change the Random class to take an signed int for the seed (it currently takes an unsigned int) and change the default, "random" value from `0` to `-1`. This way we can stick with plan but have to change the signature of Random.

There are other options as well but these seem to be the two that deviate the least. I think 1 makes a little more sense but would like to hear from @numenta/nupic-reviewers (in particular @subutai )

More generally speaking, I don't think we should have formatting requirements regarding principals.
If we want to keep authentication pluggable, we should make no assumptions on formatting.

For example, we should not expect ``system:everyone``, nor ``group:<group_id>`` to be among principals.

I believe it should be the other way around : it will be the role of our authorization policy to transform and compare native Pyramid principals (and result of group finder function) to the principals required for a particular operation on a particular resource.

See your point about the errors @lnielsen, if a missing volume would throw an error and break compilation. Usually, it's just a warning so it's not that bad. Why wouldn't I file issues? I'd have to check all doctypes in export, add missing fields, cross check, most likely in this case I'll be faster to do it myself than export first and fix it up. (IOW: you'd loose me very fast on this. To fast to file issues. But that's just a guess.)

But as I say: the main issue is not a document type or whatever but who defines the document type for export.

We use the users input decision, your procedure prefer to use existing metadata and guess what it could be. We prefer to warn the user if input is missing, you prefer not to and get clean compilation on output.

Both are valid procedures in a sense.

If I look at what you do for submission to Zenodo I see why you do the guessing. Your type _Publication_ is almost everything (interesting that you split of poster and presentation, BTW), so your submission just don't ask the user what it is, and thus you don't even stand a chance to tell which fields should be filled in. (Actually, I still believe that this is the only reason to have document types at all.) So, at Zenodo AFAIS I can fill in all journal fields, all book fields, all theses fields and all conference fields for one single publication or I can also leave everything blank even for the mentioned proceedings paper published in a journal. So indeed you'll end up with a lot of `@misc`, I guess. E.g. 16697 says "book section" and instead of `@inbook` it gets `@misc`. 16696 says journal article and again gets `@misc`, while 16694 says journal article and gets `@article` and so on. This would indeed be (unusable) inconsistent in _our_ world which has to produce the scientific report at the end of the day with a proper bibliography. Zenodo doesn't want to do that, however. (BTW: I think if it displays journal article as a badge it should export `@article` or display misc on the badge as well to get consistent. At the moment two different guesses get different results. Probably a ticket for Zenodo?)

So, I admit, having these extremes you're right: you can't do it via a general export and every instance will forever end up with needing programmers to adopt source code.

I doubt it as numexpr focuses on principle operations.  Such a thing as binning data isn't on the radar at the moment, as dimension reduction operations are tougher to multi-thread efficiently.  A multi-dimensional histogram would have to be implemented by hand in C, I think, probably using something like the GNU Scientific Library implementation as a template.

What I would suggest is you take a look at Numba to write such an algorithm.  It's a just-in-time compiler that can translate pure python into efficient byte-code.  That may be more practical.

    "@context": [ "site_context.jsonld" ,

I believe your usage will work and be fully compliant.

- let's say dx=1, i.e., you shift by 1 in sky coordinates.

- but since you use drawImage without specifying a drawing method, it's convolving with a top-hat pixel response before drawing.

All right, now I got what your problem is... It is about `wal_archive` on the master...

`archive_cleanup_command` command is never executed on the master. It is executed only on replicas.
There is no point to have archiving enabled, because nobody is reading from `wal_archive` anyway.
Either you should disable `archive_mode` completely or set `archive_command: "true"`.

Discussion from PR #500:

 ants 6 days ago Contributor

Doing this as a callback is fragile, If the API call fails the cluster is effectively unavailable until failover is externally triggered.

Also, there is no interlock to prevent racy updates. Imaginary scenario:

    unhealthy cluster
    n-0 acquires leader lock, starts callback.py
    n-0 hangs for a period of time (IO system problems, network issues, etc.)
    n-1 acquires leader lock, starts callback.py
    n-1 callback.py completes, directing endpoint traffic to n-1
    n-0 becomes active again, runs the API call and overwrites the endpoint with n-0
    n-0 demotes itself
    until next failover traffic is now routed to the standby.

I think this needs integration with the HA loop to fix properly.

@CyberDem0n
CyberDem0n 5 days ago • edited Member

In order not to have a race conditions master endpoint should be changed only by leader.
Current implementation is using ConfigMaps to store information about leader. The same way we can use Endpoints instead. I.e. Patroni can periodically (every HA loop) update annotations and subsets of the leader Endpoint.

Off course such solution has it's own pros and cons.

Pros: when the new master get a leader lock it will atomically change master service

Cons:

    I have no idea how kubernetes will react on periodic updates of leader Endpoint. Every update in theory should cause update of leader Service. We should really check that it will not trigger rebuild of iptables rules everywhere.
    Right now Patroni is doing 2 API reads (list_namespaced_config_map and list_namespaced_pod). New implementation will do 3 calls (list_namespaced_endpoint). We kind of can avoid it by converting all ConfigMaps to Endpoints, but I am not sure that it is a good idea.

@ants
ants 5 days ago Contributor

Directly patching Endpoint has another downside - OpenShift does not let normal users make endpoints that point to pods. As documented in the final paragraph here. At least for OpenShift I would like to rely on pod annotations and have the platform take care of routing a Service to the master. But even that still has the same issue that for reliability a callback is less than optimal.
@jberkus
jberkus 5 days ago Contributor

I'm nervous about the idea of dropping the leader lock if grabbing the endpoint/service fails. That ups the number of sequential operations which need to succeed to three; that seems prone to race-condition type failures.

Some unresolved questions:

    What should be the proper ordering of leader lock, failover/promotion, endpoint?
    What's the sequence of undoing these actions if any of them fails? What happens if the undo fails?
    What do we want to be the result if kubernetes is unresponsive/misconfigured and no node can update the endpoint?

That last case is potentially pathological, and argues that updating the endpoint needs to come before failover/promotion instead of afterwards, since changing the endpoint is easily reversable and failover is not. If we keep it afterwards, having RBAC config issues or container networking issues could mean that you have a set of disconnected nodes which can't be ever reconnected as replicas, and among which Patroni can't choose a good new master candidate.

I think this needs a deeper discussion than a line-item comment on a patch review.

Hi Alex, thanks for sharing this,
For the backup storage, is there a way to just use a local folder?
If we use k8s volume-mount to a directory instead of a specific bucket we can allow k8s to abstract the actual volume used (can still use gce/s3 buckets, but also all other volume-backends k8s supports) and avoid the cloud-vendor dependency altogether.

Patroni already monitors replication lag to make sure stale replicas don't get promoted. Dropping is a different issue. Why would someone want to drop a stale replica from cluster and what would it entail? The best that Patroni could do is to shut the server down to avoid wasting resources. For a completely automated cloud one would want a higher level supervisor that determines that one server is unhealthy, adds a new server and removes the unhealthy server. Something like Kubernetes.

to sum up : hbmqtt is a MQTT broker (messaging server) which uses websockets for implementing the server side. A problem is reported by @vasco-santos reported when using MQTT.js client from chrome browser : websocket connection to the broker doesn't work, the connection is closed. Same code runs fine from with firefox or safari (connection is established and data flows). So we first thought of a chrome related problem, but guys from MQTT.js say their code works fine with chrome and some other MQTT broker implementation. So back to hbmqtt problem...
My analysis is that the error comes from [this line](https://github.com/beerfactory/hbmqtt/blob/develop/hbmqtt/adapters.py#L87) in hbmqtt which receives incoming data. For some unknown reason this line throws a `ConnectionClosed` exception when the connection comes from MQTT.js/chrome. If the connection comes from MQTT.js/firefow for example, I can read data. 
I guess there may be an problem in websockets with chrome websocket implementation. Are you aware of that ?

@kylegibson I have news (and good one), to improve the situation.

- The DNS lookup done by `urllib3` uses `getaddrinfo` which uses timeouts and retries directly from `/etc/resolv.conf`. The good news is that those values can be read from the environment. I will push a PR tomorrow that sets those values in the process environment. this will avoid this 20 seconds delay, and instead drop at about 2

- I will implement a timer to blacklist the host for several minutes after an unsuccessful requests. This will avoid having this timeout on *all* the queries, but instead only every few minutes.

It's been interesting to see what is relevant to other people. From the distinction between model vs reconstruction. To a lack of need for an objective function. 

I would put more weight into hard tests such as balanced biomass equation and ensure the model is "[energetically sound]"(https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2214030116300141) for my own work. Addressing these larger/integrated issues requires that the smaller / detailed issues are okay.

But I understand not everyone is doing the same work. And one way around this issue I have heard from Christian is that memote can simply offer a suite of tests for the end-user rather than a full barrage of tests for each reconstruction.

If we are still talking about hard/soft tests, I think having metabolite annotations is the most important test memote can have, regardless of your application. Let me explain with an analogy first.

Librarians always say that if you take a book off the shelf you should never put it back yourself. The reason being you could put it in the wrong place it basically is lost and doesn't exist.

The same idea is kind of true with metabolite IDs. If your reconstruction has a metabolite ID that isn't mapped to a database (KEGG, SEED, ChEBI, METACYC), does it really exist? If metabolites are mapped to a database you can:

- do mass and charge balances on reactions
-map reactions to reaction databases using metabolite ID's (this is something I have done with several models since some of the models didn't have standard reaction ID's)
-map reactions to subsystems

There obviously shouldn't be a penalty on choosing metabolite ID's from KEGG over ChEBI, although some databases are more comprehensive. I think there is value in keeping them consistent as possible - and if I had my way I would have a penalty for not having consistent ID's.  The curator may understand the metabolite he/she created in the moment, but there is a cost to the end-user who likely has to perform a couple of Google searches to figure out what the metabolite ID is.  This can put an incentive on the model curator to submit new metabolites to database curators, rather than to wait for a database curator to add them (ie push vs pull).


In terms of numbers /tests:

- % of metabolites mapped to a database. You obviously want to maximize this.
-% of metabolites in the default namespace (ChEBI would be the default if you had 90% ChEBI vs 75% KEGG). Also max this.
-% of reactions mapped to a database (either via metabolite ID's or curator's own reaction ID's)

I would flag/fail reactions that are not mass or charge balanced.  But this is only relevant if you have metabolite information populated.

Yes, I see your point that some people may choose to generate SBML from their
model development tool and put that within a version control system, however,
that approach is quite problematic.  The reason is that when a given tool generates an
SBML export, there is no guarantee that portions of the file will be in the same order
as in the previous export.  Hence, a small edit to the model could yield a file in which
the majority of its contents have been scrambled from the previous version, yielding
a huge number of differences and making the version control system useless.

I've been thinking about this a bit more. Replacing `httplib2` down the entire stack is a really difficult maneuver. There's a lot of users of `oauth2client` and a few (such as `google-api-python-client`) that aren't maintained well enough to make this easily feasible. I don't personally have enough time to dedicate to refactoring `httplib2` out of several libraries that I don't directly maintain. I'm unsure where to go from here, but I still feel strongly that `httplib2` isn't the right choice for this library.

So I'm open to thoughts from the maintainers (and @waprin).

Some off-the-cuff ideas, half-baked, possibly terrible ideas:

1. Once `oauth2client` has 100% coverage, fork it into `oauth2client-urllib3`. Depend on that instead.
2. Switch this library to `urllib3`, leave `oauth2client` alone, re-implement the OAuth2 auth, retry, and refresh logic in our `Connection` class or via a urllib3 `Retry` class.
3. Switch this library `requests` and `requests-oauthlib`, write an adapter to translate `oauth2client` credentials. Requests is far more high-level and may be overkill.

I personally like option 2 right now, but I could be swayed.

Another thing we should do is switch classes like `pubsub.Connection` to *not* inherit from the base connection class. Instead, it should have an instance of the base connection. This way users (and us) can easily swap out the base connection.

What the BLEU score means depends what you are computing it on. If you are referring to the BLEU scores logged during training, these are BLEU values computed on the preprocessed representation of your data (possibly byte-pair encoded), so they should not be compared to 'official scores' such as generated by sacrebleu on detokenized (and then internally tokenized) data.

Orthogonally to this, BLEU scores reported by Sockeye during training are in `[0,1]`, so `0.22` doesn't sound too bad (i.e. 22 BLEU).

Training time depends on a variety of factors, such as the number and type of GPUs, stopping criteria, and validation data. If you train on a single K80 for example, you should probably expect at least 3-4 days I would say.

Let me explain why this bug is tricky to fix. When you deploy an API with SAM, it will create a `AWS::ApiGateway::Stage` resource to setup the actual stage, and an `AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment` resource to deploy the APIs to the stage.

Here is an example Deployment resource.

```
"ServerlessRestApiDeployment127e3fb911": {
      "Type": "AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment", 
      "Properties": {
        "RestApiId": {
          "Ref": "ServerlessRestApi"
        }, 
        "Description": "RestApi deployment id: 127e3fb91142ab1ddc5f5446adb094442581a90d", 

        "StageName": "Stage"  <------ NOTE THIS LINE
      }
``` 

Notice the "StageName" property on this resource. API Gateway creates a Deployment, *and* creates a new stage called "Stage". This is in addition to the regular ("Prod") stage you wanted SAM to create. 

So the fix is easy. Remove this one line from Deployment resource, right? Not so easy after all!

ApiGateway::Deployment is an immutable resource. Removing this line will have one of two outcomes next time you deploy the stack:

1. If your stack has a change to API configuration, it will roll forward and remove the extra Stage. This is because when API configuration changes, SAM detects it using a hash of the Swagger JSON, and appends the hash to ApiGateway::Deployment resource, essentially creating a new resource and deleting the old one.
 
2. If your stack does *not* change the API configuration, stack deployment will fail because CloudFormation tries remove the StageName property from existing Deployment resource, which is immutable. So ApiGateway rejects this request, and CloudFormation subsequently fails the deployment.

Unfortunately SAM is implemented as a stateless system. It does not know the current state of any of your resources. It is simply a stateless converter from SAM to CloudFormation. So SAM cannot do cool things like "remove StageName if and only if API configuration is changing". 

### Possible Solutions

1. **Version Bump**: Create a new SAM Transform 2.0 version that is backwards incompatible with 1.0. The only difference would be removing the StageName property. Customers have to upgrade to 2.0 only when changing the API Configuration. 

2. **Internal Property**: Provide a property like `___REMOVE_STAGE_NAME`, which when set to True will remove this StageName for you. But then your template is stuck with this property forever. This is really ugly and not customer obsessed.

We don't like any of the above solutions. Both of them make you, template author, to do more work. This is the reason we haven't fixed this bug yet. 

We are working with folks internally to try and find a different solution to this problem. If you prefer either of the above solutions, or have other wild ideas, do let us know!

@evtuhovich prefetch is a clever, and expedient, feature, and it exists because of how PostgreSQL fetches WAL archives.

Basically, PostgreSQL runs the restore_command for one WAL file after another. This works, but it means that you will wait to download WAL archive 2 until after you've downloaded (and loaded?) archive 1.

You thus will delay your download of all the WAL archives, in the worst case, by the slowest download.

Hence, prefetch, which actually daemonizes a new process when the restore_command is executed, and that process goes out and tries to fetch future WAL files so that they'll be ready when it's time to restore them. (And of course, there's bookkeeping involved to make sure this process doesn't conflict with any other prefetch processes outstanding...)

@DKWoods - I rewrote the `add ParagraphFormat.tab_stops` commit and merged it here:
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/commit/1153e5bb4472d2eb2c4cfb938106ed2adb939375

You'll see it's an entirely different approach:

* There are two unit tests for ParagraphFormat.tab_stops. This is because it behaves differently on the first call than on subsequent calls (for the same ParagraphFormat object). It's a @lazyproperty, which means it only creates the TabStops object the first time it's called, then holds on to it for subsequent access.
* The main test uses mocks. A mock is a "fake" object that's not really a TabStops object in this case. The mock library provides the basic functionality and a small wrapper in tests/unitutil/mock.py makes them easier to use. You probably want to do some research if you want to understand more about how they work. Here's a good start: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mock_object
* The main reason to use a mock in this case is isolation. There's no reason for the TabStops object to actually do anything, but you can tell it's created in the right circumstances and using the right call.
* There's no implementation in the oxml layer, because there doesn't need to be to get the test to pass. All that bit comes as we get further down in the next steps.

Let me know if you need more. The mocks don't show up as much once we get to the next level because most of the properties on an object use actual XML (via cxml) to see that everything works all the way through the oxml layer.

That makes sense; perhaps a subclass of `PlaidError` is not ideal — but it could still be wrapped in a library-specific exception (for example `plaid.OperationalError` or something?) or just have it documented that `requests.RequestException` and subclasses may be thrown?

As another supporting example, we just got a bunch of `requests.ReadTimeout` exceptions that were not caught, because the current interface neither wraps them into something we expect to catch *nor* does it document that they may be thrown.

I for one am OK with either approach, but the end state that we'd like to get to is that we can catch and understand essentially all of the exceptions that may be thrown by using this library, and either retry or rethrow them as appropriate. That could be done by either wrapping them into a common type, or documenting all the ones that the user needs to watch out for (the downside of this is that it makes your internal library use part of your public API, since callers will expect to catch `requests` exceptions).

The custom error handler approach is interesting... Do you have an example of how that might look, e.g. is that pattern used in any common libraries that could be used for comparison/reference?

I've started work on `sklearn2pmml.preprocessing.ExpressionTransformer`, and the MVP implementation of it should be released later this week.

The Python side of the custom transformer class is extremely simple:
```Python
class ExpressionTransformer(TransformerMixin):

	def __init__(self, expr):
		self.expr_ = expr

	def fit(self, X, y = None):
		return self

	def transform(self, X, y = None):
		return eval(self.expr_)
```

Sample usage:
```Python
transformer = ExpressionTransformer("X[:, 0] / X[:, 1]")
X = numpy.array([[0.5, 0.5], [1.0, 2.0]])
Xt = transformer.fit_transform(X)
```

Initially, the PMML/Java side will be limited to the four basic arithmetic operations `+`, `-`, `*` and `/`. But that should be enough to satisfy this feature request.

For sure, I think it can be done. It's mainly a question of the API you want and which aspects you want pluggable.

All of the behavior we're discussing in this issue happens in these three lines:

    v = v.replace("&", "&")
    if self.escape_lt_in_attrs:
        v = v.replace("<", "<")

(link to source is [here](https://github.com/html5lib/html5lib-python/blob/984f934d30ba2fde0e9ce5d4581c73fb81654474/html5lib/serializer.py#L265
))

One possible API would be to have an instance attribute that stores the simple escapes. It could be a list of pairs `(old, new)` with name and default value like so--

    self.attribute_replacements = [
        ("&", "&")
    ].

Then the three lines up top would become:

    for args in self.attribute_replacements:
        v.replace(*args)

This approach wouldn't impact performance in any noticeable way.

Hi Zzmonica,
CodaLab runs your bundles in Docker containers, which means your runs can only use packages that are installed in the Docker image used. If you don't specify a particular Docker image, the default CodaLab image is used, which at this time does not include torch or pytorch (we're working on getting a more fully featured image out).

If you want to use PyTorch or tensorflow (or any package that's not included in the default image, you need to specify a Docker image that has these packages installed via the `--request-docker-image` command flag to the `cl run` command. 

You can use any flag from https://hub.docker.com 
For example for PyTorch, you can find the official Docker images at https://hub.docker.com/r/pytorch/pytorch
You can see the available versions under the `Tags` tab on that page. Once you know the Docker image user, name and tag you want to use, you can include it on your run like the following:
`cl run <your command> --request-docker-image pytorch/pytorch:0.4.1-cuda9-cudnn7-runtime`

Hope this helps,
Kerem from CodaLab team.

This happens even with no timeout.  The problem is that sync_table assumes the table metadata is available immediately after the table creation command returns. But in less speedy cassandra environments, there is a delay before the cassandra driver actually updates the tables dictionary.  More considerate logic would give the cluster time to complete the table creation operation by trying tables[raw_cf_name] for a period specified by the configured timeout.

This documentation should be made more clear, but the required fields for specifying input data is described here:

http://girder-worker.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api-docs.html#the-input-specification

Note a few other bits of information that aren't spelled out clearly in the docs:
* A `type` field is not required when sending an input to a task. Any supplied `type` will be ignored, since the task defines the required type of each input. However, the `format` field is required because the format is allowed to differ from the task spec. If a different format is supplied, the data will be auto-converted before running the task.
* The `mode` field is inferred in a few special cases. If there is a `url` field, the `mode` is assumed to be `"http"`, and if there is a `data` field, the `mode` is assumed to be `"inline"`.

So the following input specs are equivalent:

```
{
    'format': 'text',
    'data': 'hello'
}
```

```
{
    'mode': 'inline',
    'format': 'text',
    'data': 'hello'
}
```

As well as the following:

```
{
    'format': 'png',
    'url': 'https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/2/24/Lenna.png'
}
```

```
{
    'mode': 'http',
    'format': 'png',
    'url': 'https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/2/24/Lenna.png'
}
```

I'll submit a PR to get this documentation in there.

when using the code from a tarball / git from opt, the path of exabgp should be `/opt/exabgp/sbin/exabgp` , note that it should be sbin

As your command line is `/opt/exabgp/bin/exabgp /opt/exabgp/etc/exabgp/exabgp_foo.conf` it looks like the program was installed by pip in /opt/exabgp ( and not /opt directly )

This seems to be confirmed by the fact that your configuration file is `/opt/exabgp/etc/exabgp/exabgp_foo.conf` 

Up to now ExaBGP would only seek its configuration file in:
 - using the `ENV` environment variable
 - the `--env` configuration file was indicating.
 - `/usr/local/etc/exabgp` if the program was installed in `/usr/local` 

This should be all fixed by this patch 747026f

Hi Charles - thank you for this very good report. 

For the absence of the NOTIFICATION message. I suggest that we use a new issue (or private mail) to track this particular question as I will need to understand how the session was tear down to see if this behaviour was expected or not.

For the graceful-restart issue, ExaBGP is behaving 'as designed' (but not as expected from your POV it seems) as graceful-restart was not implemented to cover the use case your are presenting. It may however be possible but would require much development and therefore it may simpler way to achieve it using BIRD or Quagga (as a "middle" peering routers).

Graceful-restart was created to help with service announcement when not using multiple servers / HA.
When announcing a /32 from ExaBGP using a loopback IP on a server, during reboot, it then become impossible to ping the destination ( network unreachable ) as the /32 will then disappear from the routing table. Graceful-Restart was created to make sure the /32 was still routable during the reboot, and until the Adj-Rib-Out is re-sent.

As you correctly found out, ExaBGP does not keep any states between restart, the Adj-Rib-Out is lost on restart. 

Adding this persistence to ExaBGP can be done two way:

First one, the "full clean solution" :

1. a configuration option to turn it on/off per peer
2. a sqlite db and some code in rib/store.py to sync data to it
3. adding the right code to rib/store.py (mostly) to perform the reading on load / update on update
4. handle failures case (i.e.: session failing to setup on reload, which should cause a cleanup of the DB)

As ExaBGP (master) can now send update SENT to peer to the helper process, developers using ExaBGP can now make sure the Adj-RIB-Out is correctly synchronised.

The quick and dirty way would be to save the updates in "binary" form and assume that the session capabilities (such as ASN4 support) would be the same on reload.

Second one "the dirty quick hack" (my way to live):

It would simply add an option per peer to not send the EOR and then have the process always send it ( which make sense as in your scenario, it is unlikely that the configuration will contain any routes at all ).
The logic would then be moved into the helper process.

So I would ask you:
1. would the second option be acceptable
2. if so, as I am dead busy atm, could you / someone implement it and I would gladly accept a PR :wink:

Let me know what you think.

I have the hunch if I am going to work out in detail how this works, my boss would like to quote for it first. But I can solve it for my 7 masters.

All instances are within a rectangle. You can connect the masters by solid lines. So in my Tamil and Sinhala x = width, y is height. One intermediate master left, two on the right.

![interpol](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/12729011/23453865/4d957a7e-fe6a-11e6-9f11-aeb7f5ebeeb5.png)

All instances that are on a solid line are interpolated between the nearest masters. That is why the "missing master" at 151-70 has nothing to do with either 151-100, nor with 26-70 (or vice versa).
It also shows the relative ease to add a missing master. In fact, we can do this in under two seconds in GlyphsApp, as we only have to select the instance and select a "Add as master" button. However as we know from accessing instances trough Python, GlyphsApp handles them all as fractional values in the interpolations. Having a physical master adds rounding. So the output is slightly different and having additional rounding is bad: 1000 units per EM is crude as it is.

So for any instance that is within the rectangle, but not on a solid line, you need intermediate interpolation masters that are on a solid line. The additional challenge that is caused by the master without horizontal solid line, is to get an additional intermediate master at the same height.

If you just ran the example code as given, why do you have 2 things in the "all_changes" list?

Anyway, you could just always `publish` the latest state and use the "history" feature so that new clients will always get the last published state. If your state is really too big to do that, then yes you'll have to do "something" to deal with the possibility of a race between "ask for the current state" and "get some changes". That is, you can't know if another client has called "make_change" between when you asked for something and when you get the answer. In general you'll want to subscribe first, then ask for the current state but be prepared to throw away some changes (because they're on earlier versions of the state than you got in your answer).

You can do "acknowledged" publishes, but that just acks that the *router* received the publish. There is no way to get acks from each subscriber.

For your second comment, you're not mis-using the API per se, but you *can* control exactly when you subscribe to `on_delete` so you could choose to do that only after you've gotten your answer for the `list`. There's still a possible race there, though: while the router is processing `wamp.registration.list` a registration could be deleted for some reason after your answer is in-flight but before you subscribe to `on_delete`...Again, the right answer is to subscribe first but be prepared to get called before all your other async "setup" (`.list`, multiple `.get` calls) finishes.

A few things, in order of decreasing urgency:
1. I just looked back at #217 and, to my horror, the final PR had `nopython=False`, even though *the whole point of that PR* was to keep numba 0.16+ from ever falling back to Python mode. So: If your code follows my example and allocates outside of the numba function, it should be safe to use `nopython=True`, and you should turn that on in this PR.
2. This is fantastic!!!! I think that having N implementations of the same algorithm is not always desirable — think what it must be like for an FFT library developer. However, we don't: We have a single Python version that always works, plus a set of accelerated implementations that are expected to give the same answer. So I think this is really quite manageable and well worth the performance increase.
3. It would be great to require numba 0.19+ in the long run, because the code would be so much more readable if we could move the array allocation back into the numba refine functions. But I don't think there should be any hurry to do that if everything's working. Closely tracking numba development is too much of a bumpy ride for my taste. Perhaps we could revisit the issue in several months?

The API differences between the `linking.link_df` and `find_link.link_simple` are now easy to oversee:

 - `linking.link` has no equivalent function in the new implementation, as it works on lists of `PointND` objects, which are non-user friendly 'internal' datatypes
 - `linking.link_iter` also
 - `find_link.link_simple_iter` takes numpy arrays instead of lists `PointND` objects
 - `find_link.link_simple` is equivalent to `linking.link_df`
 - `find_link.link_simple_df_iter` is equivalent to `linking.link_df_iter`
 - `neighbour_strategy`: the non-default BTree has not been implemented (yet) for the new linkers
 - `diagnostics`: not implemented
 - `verify_integrity`: migrated to separate function that checks a dataframe
 - `retain_index`, `copy_features`:  indices are always retained, dataframes are never copied

I am :+1: on making the new linker the default, and keeping the old code for a while as a legacy option.

Yes, this is possible. For the first bit:

```
deisctl config platform set domain=project.example.com
```

All apps will now be displayed as running on `*.project.example.com`, including deis, which will be accessible at `deis.project.example.com`. You'll need to set up DNS as described [here](http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/managing_deis/configure-dns/#necessary-dns-records) for the platform domain, changing `*.myapps.com` to `*.project.example.com`.

As for the specific app, you can just use a one-off domain via `deis domains:add project.example.com`.

Hope this helps!

This is fixed in version 1.2.0. If you still have any issues let me know. Here is the pr containing the fix (and other fixes)

https://github.com/ambitioninc/django-regex-field/pull/30

Django converts the field from a string to a regex before running validators so it is trying to run a max length check on a regex object. It looks like django adds a default max length validator internally even if you don't specify one. The django admin form must call .clean on the model, which tries to run validators. My fix for this was to override the run_validators method to call the parent method with the string representation of the regex.

I should add this is not an issue with [conda]( https://github.com/conda/conda ), but with [conda-build]( https://github.com/conda/conda-build ), which contains all of the building capabilities.

Regarding giving good hints, I agree. I always found that to be the hardest problem, way harder than solving the package specification problem itself. The best I ever came up with was showing which packages conflicted, which got the obvious cases out of the way (like no Python 3 support) and at least gave people a reasonable starting ground for the less obvious ones. 

In one of the papers for one of the other SAT solver package managers (I forget which one) they mentioned that their "rule" approach to solving package dependencies allowed them to give users good reasons why unsatisfiable dependencies were unsatisfiable. This was my original idea behind the `--alt-hint` (which also doesn't do what it used to do btw), which gave a minimal unsatisfiable subset of the clauses themselves. But unfortunately, the clauses don't directly translate to "package rules" so well, and except for simple cases (like `conda install python=2 python=3`) you would get about a hundred clauses and the actual cycle in them for why things didn't work would be hard to follow (even for simple cases like this "no Python 3 hyde" example). But a possibility would be to actually formulate package "rules" in a higher level way, which users could easily understand, and show a minimal subset of them. I guess the trick is somehow abstracting away the complexity of the package solving of "X" means "X 1.0 or X 1.1 or X 1.2 or ..." and "X 1.0 doesn't work because ..." and "X 1.1 doesn't work because ..." and ..., which is technically how things work, but isn't how users tend to think of things.

Also, what you have here seems promising, but it needs to be reduced, as I said above.

Seems like https://github.com/conda/conda/commit/24ad4709#diff-e524c3264718c66b34ea0dadbfc2559fR88 introduced the bug. 

The request header sent by `requests.session` in this case has:
```bash
'Accept-Encoding': 'gzip, deflate', 'Accept': '*/*'
```
AFAIK, the `Content-Length` header holds the raw length of the HTTP body. So, in this case, it will show 195 bytes, which is the size of the compressed 310 bytes.

If you do: `print(len(resp.raw.data))` you will see 195.

It seems the `javareconf` that is being run during conda initalization (like so: `R CMD javareconf`, in `anaconda3/etc/conda/activate.d/activate-r-base.sh`) fails to move the new `Makeconf` and `ldpath` files that are create due to differing permissions (see Errors below), as we need to have `rw-rw-r--` permissions in our shared anaconda3 folder. 

Now I solved it temporarily by making a very small change to `anaconda3/lib/R/bin/javareconf`, on line 414:

```
- mv -fv "${file}.new" "${file}"

so it justs overwrite the old files with the new without prompting, but writing a line about it into the log output.

While none are as good as IGV in the general case, they have many strong features for specialized cases. The bulk of the events that we will confidently interpret as causative today do fall into a limited number of specialized cases. 

IGV is still going to be much needed for interspersed duplications, inversions with a little more than the most common complications and more complex rearrangements.

I’d say samplot or svviz is a good substitute for the sv aspect of pileup.js, but IGV covers that as well as more complex cases - and the SNV role of pileup. Still, none of the above is really good at e.g. insertions.
Tell me if we should show this somehow, or is this only for internal purposes?

Hi, the issue is the lower-case g in the MD-tag, only the following characters are allowed according to the SAM spec:


MD:Z:
[0-9]+(([A-Z]|\^[A-Z]+)[0-9]+)*
String for mismatching positions.
```

```

It would be extremely trivial to add another line:

As strange as this may sound, `--loglevel DEBUG` does not behave the same way as `--debug`.  You may see more with the `--debug` flag.

The reason for the discrepancy is that there's a check for the `--debug` flag that enables other logging, where this is not true for merely setting `--loglevel DEBUG`. Hi @optlink 

Yes, the rc7 is planned. The problem is still that for a meta-package to be tagged as "stable", I need all sub-dependencies as stable. And it's not the case currently.

However, if you do packages for the CLI, the CLI uses the same behavior: they are cut into services and sub-packages . For instance, [this is the Network implementation of the CLI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/azure-cli-network/2.0.1). And this package follow directly a specific version of Network (this 2.0.1 is linked to 0.30.0). So even if I do a rc7, I can't assure that at all packages will match all the sub-dependencies of the CLI. And even if I assure it today, this can change tomorrow with an update of Network or something else.

I'm not sure I understand clearly your constraints (I'm a Ubuntu user, I just know Arch by name sorry :-( ), but send me an email at MS (\<githubalias\> at microsoft.com) and we will discuss with the CLI team how to help, or at least brainstorm something.

FYI @derekbekoe @johanste

There is an interface you can use to provide the signature to the request, which is close to the behavior asked here. The class must provide an implementation of the `sign_request` method:

```python
class ServiceBusCustomAuthentication:
    def sign_request(self, request, httpclient):
        # Example when adding an header, but you can do anything you want with the request
        request.headers.append(
            ('Authorization', 'my sensitive authentication string')
        )
```

Then, create the `ServiceBusService` using an instance of this class:
```python
client = ServiceBusService(authentication=ServiceBusCustomAuthentication())
```

One more thing to note: both the .bullet serialization and the new physics-client-server API share one important purpose: being able to easily reproduce problems/issues. Before I had the .bullet file format, it took a lot of effort/time to reproduce issues of developers, who integrated Bullet into their app (game, 3d modeler, movie plugin, robotics simulator like gazebo, ROS app etc). With the .bullet format, people can send me the .bullet file, and I can load it into my app (ExampleBrowser) to debug and visualize and see what is wrong. The physics client-server goes one step further: if you integrate the new physics API into an app (like gazebo, game, Blender etc), and you have some issue, you can 'connect' remotely to my machine over UDP or TCP, and I can put breakpoints, track down the issue, without having to touch the client code base (gazebo, Blender, ROS are huge difficult to debug beasts). I realize a lot of the rationale behind recent Bullet developments may not make much sense for a new developer looking into the code.

I thought having explicit names was better than nested scopes because it is clear to read.

I'm fine to go back to the first ChainMap design for `match_info`.
The problem is `url_for()`.

To allow use `url_for()` in the innermost ("leaf") app *without* knowing whatever variables are included in its outer subapp prefixes, then I think we need a strong assumption that `url_for()` is only used inside request handlers where `match_info` is available in the current task-local context.
Once `url_for()` has access to the nested `match_info`'s ChainMap then it can fill the prefix variables using the matched values of the current request's path.

Still we have two issues:

1. Python asyncio currently lacks task-local context support which automatically propagates to subtasks. To workaround this, it may be necessary to change the `url_for()` API to accept the `request` object (since it plays the role of handler-local context now) or its `match_info` attribute. But this would break use of `url_for()` in template rendering.
2. `url_for()` may be used to refer URLs with prefixes other than the current request's path has. In this case users need to provide the prefix variables anyway, like my "name." design (of course the concrete method may be different).

This was mostly an implementation focused thought. When I looked at implementing formgrade as a JupyterHub service here is roughly what I found:

* nbgrader's current auth stuff is very complicated. I have spent probably 3 hours at this point trying to understand how it all works and still don't understand enough to work on this part of the code base.
* Almost 100% of the complexity in the auth/deployment comes from the live notebook stuff in formgrade.
* Without the live notebook stuff, the conversion of formgrade to a service would be really easy (I could probably do it in < day) and lead to a dramatic reduction of code/complexity in nbgrader.
* Keeping the live notebook stuff (implemented in the same way), will require a much larger time investment and would maintain the complexity of the current design.

The complexity of formgrade really scares me. It is part of the project I would like to be able to contribute to in the long run, but don't feel able to at this point because of the complexity (at least the auth/deployment part of it). I don't want to make assumptions that someone else will always be there to debug these issues at the 11th hour before classes starts. Not being sure you would be around to help (I am deeply grateful you have been!), I had already started to investigate alternate approaches that would allow me to abandon formgrade completely. Because of this vulnerability I tend to favor more pedestrian solutions (with fewer features) that I understand. This also applies to other instructors trying to deploy this stuff. I have the deployment of JupyterHub very well worked out (in jupyterhub-deploy-teaching) - expect for the formgrade stuff - it is by far the most complex and fragile part of the deployment. With that situation, it is really hard to recommend it to other instructors who aren't or have access to a dev ops person.

I want to emphasize that the functionality of formgrade is really solid. There are still rough edges with nbgrader (such as how to send feedback to users, or how to best write and grade various types of problems) but that is mostly independent of formgrade. I would just love to find a way to simplify its authentication and deployment story.

4. If one day we do switch to a server written in e.g. node or Go, I don't think it would be all that hard to update nbextensions for another kind of discovery.

Hi, I had a similar issue where I was doing physics simulations that involved complex numbers. I got around this by using - which seems to be working okay for me but you should double check.

```
from autograd.core import make_vjp as _make_vjp, make_jvp as _make_jvp
from autograd.extend import primitive, defvjp_argnum, vspace
from autograd.wrap_util import unary_to_nary
from autograd.builtins import tuple as atuple

@unary_to_nary
def jacobian_pkl(fun, x):
    vjp, ans = _make_vjp(fun, x)
    ans_vspace = vspace(ans)
    jacobian_shape = ans_vspace.shape + vspace(x).shape
    grads = map(vjp, ans_vspace.standard_basis())

    grads_out = np.stack(grads)
    if(np.prod(jacobian_shape) == np.prod(grads_out.shape)):
        return np.reshape(grads_out, jacobian_shape)
    else:       
        my_jacobian_shape = ans_vspace.shape + vspace(x).shape + (2,) # 2 to support real/im        
        re_im_grads = np.squeeze(np.reshape(grads_out, my_jacobian_shape))
        out = re_im_grads[..., 0] + 1j * re_im_grads[..., 1]         

        return out
```

In my case I also had some affine transformations with complex numbers. If that is what you have, you can modify your function to take in a vector with the real and imaginary components stacked, and do the complex math within the function - that way the jacobian never sees a complex number.

I think you want the `np.linalg.inv` function, and you can get the autograd-wrapped version this way:

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
np.linalg.inv  # <- you want to call this function!
```

The matrix inverse function is differentiable for nonsingular matrices. You can find a brief discussion on p. 644 of [Boyd and Vandenberghe's Convex Optimization](http://web.stanford.edu/~boyd/cvxbook/bv_cvxbook.pdf). The basic idea is that if you think in terms of forming a local linear approximation to the inverse function at a point X in its domain, you can write (X + dX)^{-1} ≈ X^{-1} + X^{-1} dX X^{-1}, which suggests that if f(X) = X^{-1} then its Jacobian is given by ∂f(X)(Y) = X^{-1} Y X^{-1}. Because autograd uses reverse-mode autodiff, [we implement the adjoint of that linear operator, which basically means adding transposes](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/dc78f10b9b616654d69e39df38239107ed9b6250/autograd/numpy/linalg.py#L26).

Even autograd.builtins.tuple could break some stuff, a simple example would be something like
```python
from autograd.builtins import type
def f(x):
    t = (x, x, x)
    assert type(t) is tuple
    return sum(t)
```

Your problem is - as the error you post is saying - that `grad` is a module that differentiates scalar-output functions, and you are applying it to `df` which is vector-output (since your original function takes in two inputs).  You need to use another module from the `autograd` library to differentiate `df`, for example `jacobian`.   Like this

```
# import autograd functionality
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
from autograd import jacobian

# define function and derivatives
f = lambda w: w[0]**2 + w[1]**2
df = grad(f)
ddf = jacobian(df)
```

#### Multiple modules with the same name

You If you have multiple modules named exactly the same thing, one of them will override the other, just like you're seeing in your example. This is happening because the YAML file gets parsed into a dictionary, and dictionaries can only have one value for a given key. Unfortunately, as far as I know, there aren't any YAML parsers for Python that can recognize this error and report it; the one we use just silently overwrites the first value with the second. Because of all that, you always have to give your modules different names.

To help other people hitting the same problem, I think we should do this: After we parse everything out and get the module and rule names, read through the file and see if there's just more than one line that starts with that full name (e.g. `git module X`). If we find more than one, we can print a helpful warning.

#### Importing several modules to the same place with their own subdirectories

The way peru interprets import paths is that the *contents* of a module go at that path. No extra subdirectory is created other than what you put in the import path yourself. So (including the fix above about using different module names) you might want your `peru.yaml` file to look something like this:

```
imports:
  minify-plugin: plugins/minify
  reference-plugin: plugins/reference

git module minify-plugin:
  url: https://github.com/rdegges/pelican-minify

git module reference-plugin:
  url: https://github.com/mitchtbaum/pelican_reference
```

Note the explicit `minify` and `reference` directories in the import paths. As you've noticed, when you leave that off, files go straight into the `plugins` directory. (Sometimes this is useful, like when a module is just one file.)

#### Other questions you kinda sorta alluded to

**Why does the \* end up in the directory name in the first example?** Stars don't have any special meaning in the import path, so they're interpreted as a character in the path just like any other. (Note that stars are special in some other fields, like `pick`.) Imports are always written with one module name on the left, and one path on the right.† The reason that `mkdir *` doesn't seem to work is that the shell always interprets stars specially, so if you want to create a directory with stars in the name, you need to do something like `mkdir '*'`.

**How does peru merge files when two imports conflict?** It doesn't; this is always an error. In fact it's also an error when imports conflict with other files that peru didn't even create. Better to be safe and fail fast.

**Does tar create a directory for each archive you unpack?** Surprisingly no, tar doesn't necessarily do that. Tar will create a subdir if the files in the tarball say they belong in that subdir, but it can also dump a bunch of files into the current directory. (That's called a ["tarbomb"](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tar_%28computing%29#Tarbomb), and it's considered very bad manners.) 

† I lied :) You actually can import one module in multiple places, by putting a YAML list of paths on the right instead of a single path. But that's undocumented and usually not what you want, and in any case it still doesn't work with stars.

UPDATE: I think I found the problem---there's a race between `atlasdb_set_zonefile_present()` and `atlasdb_cache_zonefile_info()` that can cause `AtlasZonefileCrawler.set_zonefile_present()` to believe that a zone file the peer received was already present when in reality it arrived at around the same time that `atlasdb_cache_zonefile_info()` got called.  The code in `atlasdb_set_zonefile_present()` updates a zone file's "present" flag in the DB, it updates the "present" bit in the RAM-cached zone file inventory, and it returns whether or not the bit was previously set.  If `atlasdb_cache_zonefile_info()` is called at the same time, it can set the RAM-cached zone file inventory *just after* `atlasdb_set_zonefile_present()` updates the database, but *just before* `atlasdb_set_zonefile_present()` checks the in-RAM copy to see if the zone file was present.  This will cause `atlasdb_set_zonefile_present()` to report the zone file as already present, even though it was not.  This in turn stops the `SubdomainDB.enqueue_zonefile()` method from being called.

You can verify the buggy behavior by comparing a subdomain database generated from a node on spin-up against a subdomain database generated by blowing away the sudomain database and forcing it to be regenerated from scratch.  The regenerated subdomain database will have all the subdomain entries, and the subdomain database created by the spin-up will be missing a few zone files (like 5-ish), since a few of the zone files will get omitted from subdomain processing due to the above race conditions.

You keep saying lexical order but it sounds like you want them to be returned in the order they were put in the source file. I don't think this definition would be sound (consider partial classes and code generation).

My opinion is that the order is generally undefined. This ensures we don't spend additional time sorting in case you don't care about sorting or need to apply your own sorting. Also, there are many ways to order members. For instance, many people want something like this:

* Order types by name and then by generic arity
* sort by kind (constructors, methods, properties, events, fields)
* Within each group, order by name and then by arity

I agree it's a useful thing to add and could be an extension methods over, say `IEnumerable<MemberInfo>`.

NET461 used to be `netstandard1.4` but the latest version of NuGet remapped that to `netstandard2.0`.  It looks like you're using that new version of NuGet with the old packages.

The older packages (like the System.Runtime 4.3.0 package) have a mismatch when installed on .NET 4.6.1.  This is because they have a version of their library for net461 that matches the version that was in the netstandard1.4 folder, but now that NuGet allows netstandard1.6 libraries to install they are broken.

We aren't shipping new versions of these packages, the intent is for folks to get the latest facades from the tooling.

Can you try the following:
1. set `DependsOnNETStandard` in your project and see if it fixes it.
2. If not, then reference the latest `NETStandard.Library.NETFramework` package in your net461 project.

The session timeout looks incorrect, it is usually not possible to set it under 6000ms:
`    'session.timeout.ms': 298,` 

And I'm suspecting the very high fetch timeout is causing enqueued requests to time out, is there a reason you are setting it this high? Since the same broker connection will be used for other requests and the Fetch request is blocking it is not recommended to have a value higher than say 1000ms for this:

`'fetch.wait.max.ms': 60000,`

You should leave the heartbeat at its default value (which will then be set to a third of the session.timeout.ms) or make sure that the value is well below the session timeout):

`'heartbeat.interval.ms': 60000,`

Another thought here is that right now the `zoneinfo` module is a bit weird because we always read the `tzfile` objects from a tarball, which actually has some consequences for the interface.

I think the reason for this is that we try to be agnostic as to whether this is a zip install or not, and we can't guarantee that there's an actual on-disk location, but we *can* use the `pkgutil` facilities to guarantee that we have the *data* that gets shipped with the package. I'd like to re-examine the options here, because it is *very* tempting to just install the zoneinfo data somewhere and append that to the existing `TZPATH`.

Hi Jianfen,

The genomic variant descriptions being returned by hgvs are correct. HGVS nomenclature guidelines state that deletions must be described in their most 3 prime position with respect to the specified reference sequence. The positions in column 1 of your table are equivalent in terms of the deleted bases but are the most 5 prime positions possible for the deletion (similar to VCF).

Hope this helps.

Thanks

Pete

And this is the full code of my serializer:

    from rest_framework_json_api import serializers
    from .models import Account
    from . import validators
    class AccountSerializer(serializers.ModelSerializer):
        """
        A serializer for `accounts.models.Account` model.
        """
        confirm_password = serializers.CharField(
            write_only=True,
            max_length=128,
            min_length=8,
            required=True
        )

        class Meta:
            model = Account

            exclude = (
            # exclude admin fields
                'is_staff',
                'is_superuser',
            )
            read_only_fields = (
                'username',
                'date_joined',
                'last_login',
            )
            extra_kwargs = {
                'username': {'validators': [validators.username_validator]},
                'email': {'validators': [validators.email_validator]},
                'password': {'write_only': True}
            }

        def create(self, validated_data):
            # remove confirm_password field
            validated_data.pop('confirm_password')

            user = super().create(validated_data)
            user.set_password(validated_data['password'])
            user.save()
            return user

        def validate(self, data):
            """
            Extra validation (i.e. passowrds match)
            """
            validators.passwords_match(data)
            return data

        def get_fields(self, *args, **kwargs):
            fields = super().get_fields(*args, **kwargs)
            request = self.context.get('request', None)

            # Enables editing `username` field if it's POST request and remove
            # `password` field if it's not.

Here we are talking about interpolants, not models. For the extension array theory I have been allowing array-ext in interpolants since in practice this worked better than the alternative (replacing occurrences of array-ext with instances of its definitional axiom). Having said that I have found that it is better to switch array extensionality off when it is not needed, which is a common case in program analysis (at least for C-like languages with no array comparison operator). 

If it's useful, we could add an option to replace phi(array-ext(a,b)) with this:

exists x. (select(a,x) != select(b,x) \/ a = b) & phi(x)

I don't know how useful this would be in practice. An alternative would be to make array-ext available in smt2. 

A little more information about the application would help to decide what is most useful.

In principle, that's easy to do:

1. With `params = lasagne.layers.get_all_param_values(network, trainable=True)`, obtain all parameter values.
2. Concatenate all of them into a vector: `v = np.concatenate([p.ravel() for p in params])`.
3. Create a shared variable from that vector: `v = theano.shared(v)`.
4. Map each parameter to a slice of that shared variable:
```python
slices = {}
a = 0
for param, value in zip(lasagne.layers.get_all_param_values(network, trainable=True), params):
    b = a + value.size
    slices[param] = v[a:b].reshape(value.shape)
    a = b
```
5. Now it gets a little hairy. You need to replace all parameters referenced by the network by the slices. You can either do this with `theano.clone` on the loss expression, or try to modify the network:
```python
for layer in lasagne.layers.get_all_layers(network):
    for p in layer.params:
        if p in slices:
            layer.params[slices[p]] = layer.params.drop(p)
    for k, p in list(layer.params.__dict__.items()):
        if p in slices:
            setattr(layer, k, slices[p])
```
6. You can verify the result with something like `assert all(p.owner.inputs[0] is v for p in lasagne.layers.get_all_params(network, trainable=True))`.

Untested, but should work unless you use recurrent layers, which tuck away the parameters a bit better. In that case, you'll need a custom weight initialization class that hands out slices to a symbolic vector. (In case you don't want a shared variable for the values, but a symbolic vector that becomes an input to the Theano function, omit step 3 above, rename `v` to something else so you still have the initial vector values, and create `v = theano.tensor.vector()`. The rest will work unchanged.)

Closing because right now I'm treating this as a question, not as a feature request. A generic solution that works for all kinds of layers would be a nice addition to `lasagne.layers` or `lasagne.utils`, but not easy to do. It's easier to modify the Theano expressions.

Hmm, that actually doesn't look very good anyway, we don't open the files for reading only and we don't close them:
```python
here = os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__))
try:
    README = open(os.path.join(here, 'README.rst')).read()
    CHANGES = open(os.path.join(here, 'CHANGES.rst')).read()
except IOError:
    README = CHANGES = ''
```

A possible fix for your case might be:
```python
here = os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__))
try:
    with open(os.path.join(here, 'README.rst'), 'rb') as f:
        README = f.read()
    with open(os.path.join(here, 'CHANGES.rst'), 'rb') as f:
        CHANGES = f.read()
except IOError:
    README = CHANGES = ''
```

This will read the files in binary mode and thus won't try to decode anything, but the `long_description` will be of type `bytes` instead of `str` then -- is that a problem? Can you try?

The alternative would be:
```python
here = os.path.abspath(os.path.dirname(__file__))
try:
    with open(os.path.join(here, 'README.rst'), 'r', encoding='utf8') as f:
        README = f.read()
    with open(os.path.join(here, 'CHANGES.rst'), 'r', encoding='utf8') as f:
        CHANGES = f.read()
except IOError:
    README = CHANGES = ''
```

This would be incompatible with Python 2 and need some case distinction, but if it's the only way to get it to work independently of the platform's locale settings, that's the way we should go.

The `SubFS` is a new object, so it won't have the same attributes as the class it wraps. If you need to modify your AbstractFS after it has been constructed, you will need to keep a reference to it.

Picking is something that PyFilesystem had, but was intentionally not support in PyFilesystem2.

Pickling doesn't make sense for many filesystems. What should happen if you pickle a tempfs or a memoryfs for example? Should network filesystems store authentication? What happens if you unpickle an OSFS on another machine? There are too many edge cases to make it a general concept.

If you want to restore a filesystem in a replaceable way, look into [FS URLs](https://docs.pyfilesystem.org/en/latest/openers.html).

I had read this and didn't realize we had hold outs for GPL. This actually could deny access to people who don't feel like upending their way of doing things to comply with that license.

In any event, if it's closed, thanks for your participation. If @crobinso has more energy sometime in the future, maybe this can be looked at again.

-------

Re: @wgwoods (https://github.com/python-bugzilla/python-bugzilla/issues/25#issuecomment-376912981)

> So. Having thought about it a while, I concluded that - in this particular case - I'm fine with MIT, as requested and as is the de facto standard in the Python community.

Yep. That's what I'm thinking. I tend to prefer licenses that fit in with what other packages are using.

>  MPL-2.0 files in a MIT project without losing copyleft on the former or changing the license on the latter. 

I'm fine with MPL as a license by itself. In this specific instance, I think MPL could be worth discussing more, but MIT may be a better fit when it comes to the python package ecosystem.

The reason why I think this is it's since this is a library that's a client to access bugzilla, I'm more inclined that it's in a license more common among Python packages, rather than the host software itself.

>  @tony, would that work for your purposes?

So, for those curious, my purpose was wanting to create a CV portfolio of open source contributions. The hope is people with open source contributions could show off their work more conveniently.

I actually did follow through with this: https://github.com/tony/cv. Here's the GitHub scraper: https://github.com/tony/cv/blob/master/scripts/github.js

The hope is a library like this can be used to pull/sift through Bugzilla trackers. Currently, I have to add my contributions on FreeBSD's Bugzilla [by hand](https://github.com/tony/cv/blob/e6e44fc/data/my_activities.json#L85).

Re (https://github.com/python-bugzilla/python-bugzilla/issues/25#issuecomment-376856087):

One option for @crobinso and the rest of the team to consider is making a contribution guideline so future contributions are dual licensed MIT+GPL. That way if the people who felt like GPL change their mind, MIT is a viable option in the future and there's not a need to "catch-up".

> Personally I don't have strong feelings about changing the license, and I certainly don't have the desire to do anything antisocial like removing people's code to facilitate a license change, so I will be abandoning this effort and sticking with the GPL.

In the mean time, if people need a python bugzilla client, they're going to have to write their own from scratch. They're being denied service, because the license's terms aren't tenable to their situations.

The lack of accessibility due to license obligations is the real thing that's the problem. And perhaps, even a bigger political issue than removing GPL commits in some way*. At least if it's me, that's the opposite of what I'm trying to achieve with open source - I don't want to burden my users with complicated terms. Especially since viral licenses are more often used in compiled languages. That's my theory.

With MIT, it's forward compatible with GPL. So the theory is the theory is MIT could suit the needs of people who prefer GPL, while not disabling access to people who can't agree to GPL's terms.

> Maybe LGPL is a palatable option for everyone but for now I've run out of energy to consider it and chase that down

LGPL may be a fine license in itself, but it's generally not used in the Python community or scripting languages.

\* didn't SDL2 do something similar when they went from LGPL -> ISC? I am having trouble finding out how they did it

----------------

Thanks everybody for considering this license change.

I'm slowly figuring out group chat. For example, I managed to get joining group chats to work. You have to use non-protobuf messages (Msg class) serialized to bytes. For example, given an invite with chatroomid, to join that chatroom we need something like this:

```
msg = Msg(EMsg.ClientJoinChat, extended=True)
msg.body = MsgClientJoinChat(chatroomid)
client.send(msg)
```

Where MsgClientJoinChat is a class encapsulating the required fields, and providing a method for serialization to bytes. A crude version of this class could look like this:

import StringIO

```
class MsgClientJoinChat(object):
    def __init__(self, sidc):
        self.SteamIdChat = sidc
        self.IsVoiceSpeaker = False

    def serialize(self):
        out = StringIO.StringIO()
        hsteamidchat = format(self.SteamIdChat, '02x')
        if len(hsteamidchat)%4!=0:
            hsteamidchat = (len(hsteamidchat)%4)*'0' + hsteamidchat

        newstr = ""
        for i in range(len(hsteamidchat), 2, -2):
            newstr += hsteamidchat[i-2:i]

        hsteamidchat = bytearray.fromhex(newstr)
        out.write(hsteamidchat)
        out.write("\x00")
        return out.getvalue()
```

The hard part is converting the steam id into bytes and reversing their order (unless I missed some built-in python function that does that).

After sending the message in the way described in the first block of code, the bot joins the chatroom. I was unable to find non-protobuf messages with bodies specific to their types (like this MsgClientJoinChat class I pasted here). Have I missed them or is this functionality not implemented in the library?

Actually, this is an old bug that was around since the beginning, but new logic in config-ui forced it to reproduce: 
There was a suggestion some time ago (#142) to verify that when you set new value for an option it is actually being registered by immediately reading that option. If you follow that recommendation and add:
`dev->get_option(o.opt);` to config-ui.cpp after line 210 you would see the same problem. 
Now for the problem itself, it is related to how v4l is implementing asynchronous controls. In the [UVC spec, 2.4.4 Control Transfer and Request Processing](http://www.cajunbot.com/wiki/images/8/85/USB_Video_Class_1.1.pdf) it says that if a control is advertised as asynchronous and a set operation is taking more certain amount of time, the call will return before the control is actually applied. Any new requests coming before this operation completes should fail. Currently v4l does not provide a method to wait until control is actually applied, so we must work around it in software.

I have just bumped into the same issue and first thought this is a bug before I have encountered this issue.

I think this decision should be reconsidered, let me outline why:

1. When executing a mutation with a validation error it returns 200 Success status code even though there is a failure and nothing has been mutated
2. Client always has to request the errors in its query even though in general it is simply interested in the success results
3. when a graphql error occurs graphene currently returns a 400 Bad Request error which indicates that request is invalid and can be fixed. If it were a programmer error it would need to return 500 but this is not the case
4. The [specification](https://facebook.github.io/graphql/June2018/#sec-Errors) doesn't state that those are not fixable by the users (a user for me is the api client). It actually now also allows extensions to give more information to the client.

Reference for status codes: https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html

What do you think?

Using @mike42's suggestions this I changed the YAML to this:

https://github.com/miracle2k/python-escpos/blob/216184f43f2080520df7c6ab541f80d382471842/src/escpos/capabilities.yml

Notes:

1. I'm still struggling with the code pages. Why do we need to list them at all?

For example, for this Python implementation, during testing the encoder tried to use "RK1048" , which Python couldn't encode. Turns out they added that in Python 3.5. For PHP, I might compile my iconv to include some of the encodings that are currently disabled in the PHP driver. 

So again, the encodings are standardized, and what every implementation can support is up to them, and might change depending on the platform.

What good is it to list the code pages at all? Relying on the platform's encoding names works for 90% of the use cases. Except for one thing:

If my printer profile lists "I support cp437", but my user is used to providing the code page using just "437", because the platform's codecs system supports that alias, how can we find out that this encoding is supported by the chosen printer profile - since the strings don't match?

Should we, or shouldn't we, build our own "database" of encoding names and their aliases? If we don't, it's up to each implementation to either:

a) require the user to specify precisely the encoding strings that the profile uses.
b) use some other data source for encoding alias names.

NB: This only is relevant for the case that the user wants to specify an encoding manually. As long as our implementations use a "auto encode" system, the only thing that is important is that the codec system each implementation uses can resolve the "common encoding" name that the profile specifies. My guess is that we can assume that they all can deal with a name like "cp437".


2. I went on and on above about not putting an "approximate media info" or "approximate column count" inside the basic profiles, but I did here anyway. The default profile contains two fonts and column counts.

- It means that an implementor can use/expose this profiles "as-is", in the same way they would use the printer profile, and can rely on having column information, rather than having to provide their own defaults.

- By separating the base profiles and the printer definitions into two separate dicts, it's still possible to tell which information is certain knowledge (if I have a printer), and which is a guess (when I have a profile).


I'm curious what both @patkan  and @mike42 think. I'm looking forward to seeing this into the library.

It records all the interactions between napalm and the underlying library. So if napalm does `device.send_command("show int")` it will record whatever `send_command` returns (which in this case will be the raw output of the device). However, if the underlying library does any massaging to the data before sending it to us we would see the "massaged" data. This is the case for `pyez` when using the tables, for instance.

You need to format your data as follow:
```python
trainX = [
   [373 27 9 615 9 16 10 34 0 8 0 199 65917 1319 122 402 319 183],
   [77 12 4 66 4 3 0 5 0 14 3 50 106 139 38 164 53 109],
   ...
]
trainY = [3, 3, 3 ...]
trainY = tflearn.data_utils.to_categorical(Y1) # Change labels to one-hot vector
```
For a model, seems you can use the one in the [lstm example](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/nlp/lstm.py) (If these integers represent keys?) otherwise, if they are features, I suggest you normalize them by dividing all your values by the max one.

In case you have multiple labels, you can do two regressions:
```
...
net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, 2, activation='softmax')
regression1 = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam',
                                                    loss='categorical_crossentropy')
regression2 = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam',
                                                    loss='categorical_crossentropy')
net = tflearn.merge_outputs(regression1, regression2)

model = tflearn.DNN(net, clip_gradients=0., tensorboard_verbose=0)
model.fit(trainX, [trainY1, trainY2], show_metric=True, batch_size=128)
```
Result will be a merged layer of your prediction with regression1 and regression2. This can be changed according to your need (see merge layer), you could also take the average for example).
